sourceDocuments.title: Eudora Vale Senior Staff Character Bible

ship.name: U.S.S. Eudora Vale
ship.openingCondition: The Eudora Vale is completing a multiweek independent review of the Ilyra terraforming lattice. The ship is fully operational, but repeated synchronization tests have exposed main-deflector coupler variance and finite industrial feedstock. The player has served as Commander/XO long enough to know the crew when Captain Rhee dies during the opening surge.

commandStructure.playerRole: Principal mission commander for the Ilyra review; lawful Acting Captain after the opening succession.

serviceHistory.commissioning: Commissioned in 2372 as a long-range science, diplomacy, and rapid-response vessel.
serviceHistory.wartimeService: Convoy reconnaissance, medical evacuation, communications restoration, and limited strike-support missions during the Dominion War.
serviceHistory.currentAssignment: Independent technical review of the Ilyra Accord, emergency response, first-line diplomacy, and stabilization of the five-world terraforming lattice.

missionProfile: Protect civilian life throughout the Ilyra System.
Provide independent scientific assessment of the terraforming lattice and its environmental consequences.
Coordinate emergency response without replacing lawful local authority where it can function.
Preserve freedom of navigation, access to essential infrastructure, and auditable evidence custody.
Support a sustainable transition that does not make one world carry an invisible permanent burden.
Report the player captain's succession and extraordinary decisions through Starfleet command review.

keyCapabilities: High-resolution planetary, atmospheric, subspace, and biosphere sensor suites
Astrometrics laboratory configured for system-scale lattice modeling
Main deflector capable of short-duration phase synchronization with remote lattice nodes
Two aeroshuttles, four Type-9 shuttles, and atmospheric probe inventory
Advanced medical facilities suitable for population-health sampling and emergency stabilization
Industrial replicators and mobile laboratory modules for limited climate-hardware fabrication
Diplomatic conference facilities and high-capacity communications arrays
knownTechnicalDebt: Main-deflector phase couplers show heat variance after repeated lattice synchronization tests
Secondary EPS trunks on Deck 11 carry temporary bypasses from wartime repairs
Two atmospheric probe pallets require recalibration after exposure to Nacre chemistry
Industrial replicator feedstock is insufficient to fabricate complete replacement processors for all five worlds
One aeroshuttle has reduced structural margin after the Crown Station evacuation
constraints: The ship can stabilize or bridge one major lattice node at a time without unacceptable deflector risk.
Transporters and shuttles cannot evacuate a planetary population; they can preserve corridors, specialists, and high-risk communities.
The Intrepid-class science suite can identify load and consequence but cannot create political legitimacy.
Repeated high-energy intervention may advance the cascade even when the immediate mission succeeds.
Using the ship as temporary climate infrastructure reduces tactical, propulsion, and future rescue capacity.

startingStores.medicalReliefCapacity: strong for local emergencies; inadequate for system-wide displacement without regional partners

ship.playerSafeSummary: A fast, recognizable Starfleet explorer well suited to planetary science, diplomacy, emergency transport, and system-scale analysis. The Eudora Vale can understand and temporarily stabilize the lattice, but it cannot replace five worlds of infrastructure or law.

senior.name: Nasrin Rhee
senior.playerSafeSummary: A respected, cautious captain who treats negotiated access and technical patience as command tools. She leads the opening shuttle evacuation and is killed after its passengers transport clear.
senior.voice: Measured, economical, and attentive to who must live with a decision after the ship leaves. Rhee asks what can be defended publicly and what cannot yet be known.
revealGates: Her suspicion that the public lattice model omitted categories appears only through recovered logs, correspondence, or testimony.
Her agreement to delay a review in exchange for medical access to Nacre emerges after the player has independent evidence of the hidden burden.
pressureHooks: The crew invokes her precedent
Her logs complicate disclosure
Her death makes ordinary captaincy rituals politically charged
senior.directorOnly: Rhee dies in the opening after the shuttle passengers transport clear. Her death is not a hidden roll and must carry operational and personal weight. Her records provide evidence, not posthumous instructions.

senior.name: Asha Ren
senior.billet: Operations Chief and Second Officer; prospective Acting Executive Officer
senior.playerSafeSummary: Organized, direct, and politically perceptive. Ren understands how operational schedules become power and is the strongest default candidate to become Acting XO.
senior.voice: Clear, compressed, and unsentimental about institutional language. Ren identifies who owns a process, who is excluded, and what decision is being disguised as administration.
revealGates: Her worry that accepting the acting XO billet will be read as loyalty without independence surfaces after the first major disagreement with the player.
pressureHooks: Must run the ship while the player governs the system
Challenges informal commitments that lack implementation
May decline or redefine the acting XO role
senior.directorOnly: Ren supports the player's lawful succession but will not become a compliant echo. If another acting XO is chosen, she remains Operations Chief and a consequential alternative center of competence.

senior.name: Haro ch'Veth
senior.playerSafeSummary: Energetic, empirical, and impatient with ceremonial assurances. He can model the lattice at system scale but dislikes treating uncertainty as political permission to conceal data.
senior.voice: Fast, precise, and openly skeptical. ch'Veth separates measurements from policy and states confidence levels even when the room wants certainty.
revealGates: His tendency to underestimate the lethal transition between disclosure and implementation becomes visible after data publication changes behavior faster than infrastructure can adapt.
pressureHooks: Advocates broad telemetry access
Disputes secrecy with Fenn and Osei
Can overvalue elegant models
senior.directorOnly: ch'Veth can prove disparities before he can prove the original fraud. Do not let him know the twelve-year rotation without evidence.

senior.name: Milo Fenn
senior.playerSafeSummary: An imaginative systems architect with a dry manner. Fenn sees the lattice as one machine distributed across five societies and is cautious about destabilizing it faster than alternatives can be built.
senior.voice: Dry, concrete, and diagram-minded. Fenn turns policy into load paths, failure margins, fabrication hours, and the location where a promised compromise will physically break.
revealGates: He helped certify a lattice modernization six years earlier and failed to identify the permanent cost shift.
His signature appears in records only after the relevant certification chain is recovered.
pressureHooks: Needs scarce feedstock
Argues for staged change
Must decide whether to disclose his prior certification before others find it
senior.directorOnly: Fenn did not knowingly certify fraud. His review relied on curated categories. His accountability is real but distinct from deliberate concealment.

senior.name: Ila Tovan
senior.billet: Chief Medical Officer and Public-Health Specialist
senior.playerSafeSummary: Measured and attentive to population effects. Tovan treats environmental allocation as triage and resists technical language that hides who becomes sick, displaced, or infertile.
senior.voice: Quiet, exact, and difficult to deflect. Tovan distinguishes reversible discomfort, chronic harm, acute mortality, and political embarrassment.
revealGates: Her joined memory includes a prior public-health cover-up, but she does not invoke it as automatic moral authority.
She will disclose her concern only when the player asks her to endorse a mortality-obscuring allocation.
pressureHooks: Nacre registries are incomplete
Medical supplies compete with processor parts
Can refuse to certify a lethal allocation as safe
senior.directorOnly: Tovan does not decide political legitimacy. She can make mortality and health consequences impossible to hide.

senior.name: Koris zh'Raal
senior.playerSafeSummary: Disciplined, restrained, and experienced in civil disorder. zh'Raal expects sabotage and coercion to increase but does not want the Eudora Vale used as an occupying police force.
senior.voice: Controlled, specific, and procedural. zh'Raal distinguishes a dangerous person, a dangerous crowd, and an authority trying to call dissent a security problem.
revealGates: Her wartime experience with an allied security service that staged provocations surfaces after the first suspiciously convenient attack.
pressureHooks: Node seizures blur sabotage and labor action
Local security requests tactical backing
Evidence custody can conflict with rescue
senior.directorOnly: zh'Raal may recommend force but does not seek occupation. She should identify less coercive security options when they are operationally credible.

senior.name: Jaya Kel
senior.playerSafeSummary: Calm, practical, and highly familiar with orbital traffic. Kel planned the shuttle corridor used in the opening evacuation and responds to Rhee's death by trying to control every variable.
senior.voice: Brief, spatial, and practical. Kel describes vectors, windows, traffic behavior, and the one assumption in a plan most likely to become false.
revealGates: Her private belief that a different approach vector might have saved Rhee surfaces only after another pilot or the player challenges her overcontrol.
pressureHooks: Conditional routes become political chokepoints
She may resist delegating shuttle work
Orbital demonstrations complicate safe transit
senior.directorOnly: The opening flight plan was reasonable under known data. Kel's guilt is emotional and professional, not proof of negligence.

senior.name: Venn Talar
senior.playerSafeSummary: Candid, low-key, and skilled at group process. Talar warns that system consensus often means the most powerful worlds agree with one another.
senior.voice: Plain, patient, and lightly ironic. Talar names emotional facts without claiming telepathic certainty and asks who is absent from the room.
revealGates: His discomfort with institutional consensus comes from a failed mediation in which excluded workers later sabotaged a settlement, revealed only through earned rapport.
pressureHooks: Crew needs space for Rhee
Summits can become theater
Telepathic boundaries matter during high emotion
senior.directorOnly: Talar can sense broad affect with consent and species limits. He cannot read campaign secrets or substitute empathy for evidence.

supportingCrew.name: Lieutenant junior grade Sava Nirel
supportingCrew.playerSafeSummary: Maintains the shipboard lattice model and translates ch'Veth's forecasts into operational constraints.

supportingCrew.name: Lieutenant junior grade Kalen Oth
supportingCrew.playerSafeSummary: Tracks Accord authority, emergency orders, disclosure obligations, and the legal status of any successor council.

supportingCrew.name: Chief Petty Officer Bol Tressa
supportingCrew.playerSafeSummary: Maintains the feedstock ledger and can state exactly what cannot be fabricated if command approves one more emergency request.

supportingCrew.name: Lieutenant Tela Vor
supportingCrew.billet: Security Team Leader and Civil Liaison
supportingCrew.playerSafeSummary: Leads node-security and crowd-protection teams when zh'Raal must remain aboard.

cohorts.label: Rhee Command Core
cohorts.summary: Officers and enlisted personnel who served several years under Captain Rhee and know her negotiated, process-conscious command style.

cohorts.label: Postwar Science Transfers
cohorts.summary: Environmental, public-health, and systems specialists assigned for the Ilyra review.

cohorts.label: Eudora Vale Wartime Veterans
cohorts.summary: Crew shaped by rapid-response and convoy duties during the Dominion War.

cohorts.label: Recent Replacements
cohorts.summary: Personnel filling wartime losses and refit vacancies with less attachment to Rhee or the ship's established culture.

relationshipModel.startingFrame: The player has served as Executive Officer of the Eudora Vale long enough to know the crew, but assumes their first captaincy after Captain Nasrin Rhee dies in the opening lattice surge. Respect for the succession is immediate; confidence in the player's independent captaincy must be established through decisions.

campaignContext.missionProfile: Scientific review, diplomatic liaison, emergency response, and technical stabilization of the Ilyra System terraforming lattice.
campaignContext.playerRoleRule: Locked role: Commander and Executive Officer of the Eudora Vale. Captain Nasrin Rhee dies during the opening crisis, creating lawful succession to Acting Captain and the player's first independent command.

lockedRole.roleLabel: Executive Officer, principal mission commander, and Acting Captain after the opening succession
lockedRole.commandAuthority: All Starfleet mission teams, away teams, ship operations, emergency resource allocation, diplomatic representation when delegated, and full lawful command after Rhee's death.
lockedRole.captainAuthorityBoundary: Rhee retains legal command until her death in the opening mission. The player then assumes command through ordinary Starfleet succession. Starfleet may review or offer replacement, but the campaign must not remove the player from meaningful command without a consequential, player-facing process.

ageBands.label: Young for a first captaincy
ageBands.summary: A comparatively new Commander whose promotion is credible and whose succession is immediately tested.

ageBands.label: Established command-track officer
ageBands.summary: An officer with department-head, staff, and field-command experience who expected a later, more orderly first command.

ageBands.label: Late specialist transition
ageBands.summary: A seasoned expert entering line command after a long career in science, engineering, medicine, law, diplomacy, security, or operations.

allowedSpecies.label: Other Federation species
allowedSpecies.summary: Must fit 24th-century Starfleet service and may not begin with campaign-secret knowledge.

careerBackgrounds.label: Command and operations
careerBackgrounds.summary: Multi-team coordination, resource allocation, ship readiness, and emergency authority.

careerBackgrounds.label: Engineering and infrastructure
careerBackgrounds.summary: Distributed systems, damage control, fabrication, safety margins, and technical accountability.

careerBackgrounds.label: Science and climatology
careerBackgrounds.summary: Planetary systems, environmental modeling, uncertainty, and experimental intervention.

careerBackgrounds.label: Diplomacy and law
careerBackgrounds.summary: Treaties, emergency legitimacy, public process, jurisdiction, and negotiated institutions.

careerBackgrounds.label: Medical and public health
careerBackgrounds.summary: Population triage, environmental illness, consent, quarantine, and long-term harm.

careerBackgrounds.label: Security and civil order
careerBackgrounds.summary: Rules of engagement, protest protection, sabotage investigation, and proportional force.

careerBackgrounds.label: Logistics and reconstruction
careerBackgrounds.summary: Scarce resources, relief networks, fabrication priorities, and postwar recovery.

careerBackgrounds.label: Fleet staff and audit
careerBackgrounds.summary: Readiness review, oversight, doctrine, data integrity, and institutional accountability.

formativeExperiences.label: Environmental evacuation
formativeExperiences.summary: A crisis where technical success still displaced a community or damaged a living system.

formativeExperiences.label: Wartime logistics command
formativeExperiences.summary: A period when every allocation denied something necessary to someone else.

formativeExperiences.label: Treaty or coalition breakdown
formativeExperiences.summary: Experience watching a workable agreement lose legitimacy through secrecy, exclusion, or delay.

formativeExperiences.label: Labor or service dispute
formativeExperiences.summary: A command assignment where safety, authority, and collective action could not be separated.

formativeExperiences.label: Public inquiry
formativeExperiences.summary: Experience giving evidence or being reviewed after a consequential command decision.

formativeExperiences.label: Loss of a senior officer
formativeExperiences.summary: A prior succession or casualty that shaped the officer's relationship to command continuity.

formativeExperiences.label: Colony reconstruction
formativeExperiences.summary: Work where local institutions mattered more than Starfleet's ability to provide short-term relief.

formativeExperiences.label: Routine professional service
formativeExperiences.summary: A credible career without one defining catastrophe, leaving this campaign to become the officer's major test.

assignmentReasons.label: Requested by Captain Rhee
assignmentReasons.summary: Rhee selected the officer for judgment that could complement or challenge her cautious style.

assignmentReasons.label: Ilyra review specialist
assignmentReasons.summary: The officer was chosen for expertise directly relevant to the lattice or the Accord.

assignmentReasons.label: Deliberate command trial
assignmentReasons.summary: Starfleet intended the XO billet to test readiness for a later independent command.

assignmentReasons.label: Postwar promotion
assignmentReasons.summary: The officer advanced after wartime service and a shortage of experienced command personnel.

assignmentReasons.label: Outside perspective
assignmentReasons.summary: Starfleet wanted an officer without prior political commitments in the Ilyra System.

flaws.label: Overcontrol
flaws.summary: The officer has difficulty trusting delegated work when failure will be visible and irreversible.

flaws.label: Protective secrecy
flaws.summary: The officer withholds information to prevent panic or misuse.

flaws.label: Performs certainty
flaws.summary: The officer projects more confidence than the evidence supports to keep people moving.

flaws.label: Institutional reflex
flaws.summary: Formal Starfleet or Accord authority initially feels safer than contested local legitimacy.

flaws.label: Impatient with process
flaws.summary: The officer prefers operational clarity even when fair procedure is part of the solution.

flaws.label: Inherits the dead
flaws.summary: The officer treats honoring a predecessor as a duty that can obstruct independent judgment.

generationGuardrails.label: No campaign-secret knowledge
generationGuardrails.summary: The player cannot begin knowing the mandatory rotation, falsified dashboards, false flag, or Rhee's private compromise.

generationGuardrails.label: Established XO, new captain
generationGuardrails.summary: The player knows the Eudora Vale and senior staff but is assuming an independent captaincy for the first time.

generationGuardrails.label: Crew expertise preserved
generationGuardrails.summary: The player may be broadly competent; senior staff remain necessary domain experts.

generationGuardrails.label: Original identity
generationGuardrails.summary: The player is an original Starfleet officer rather than a canonical character.

generationGuardrails.label: Era fit
generationGuardrails.summary: Backstory must fit Starfleet service by stardate 55291.4.

localFallback.biographyTemplate: {{name}} is a {{species}} Starfleet Commander serving as Executive Officer of the U.S.S. Eudora Vale on stardate 55291.4. Their background in {{careerBackground}} and experience with {{formativeExperience}} made them a credible principal mission commander for the Ilyra review. Their command style is shaped by {{insightTrait}}, {{connectionTrait}}, and {{executionTrait}}, while {{flaw}} remains a pressure point as they inherit their first captaincy.
localFallback.publicReputationTemplate: {{name}} is regarded as a capable Commander whose {{careerBackground}} background fits the Ilyra review, though neither Starfleet nor the crew has yet seen how they will govern an entire system from the captain's chair.

world.title: The Ilyra System
world.summary: Five inhabited worlds and one excluded orbital habitat depend on a shared terraforming lattice that redistributes water vapor, waste heat, atmospheric compounds, and chemical burden. The public Accord calls this reciprocity; the hidden operating history made Nacre a permanent environmental sink.

travelModel.typicalTransit: Most principal locations are one to four hours apart at impulse or low warp inside the system. Conditional routes around the Harmonic Spindle and Umbra Manifold may require longer approaches, local pilots, or load windows.

everydayLife: Weather schedules are civic documents: citizens plan work, school, farming, maintenance, and public protest around allocation windows.
Every world uses different language for what it contributes and receives; those words encode political assumptions as much as technical facts.
Union technicians, shuttle crews, medical registrars, farm cooperatives, and habitat stewards carry more practical continuity than most senior officials.
The Eudora Vale remains a functioning starship with watches, meals, drills, maintenance, grief, recreation, and private relationships between emergencies.
Not every local problem is sabotage or part of the historical fraud. Old equipment, ordinary error, and incompatible priorities remain causally sufficient.
Calm scenes should allow crew mourning, technical rehearsal, cultural texture, and ordinary service without converting every pause into a countdown.

locations.title: U.S.S. Eudora Vale
locations.playerSummary: The Intrepid-class ship assigned to review the Ilyra Accord, coordinate emergency response, and provide independent scientific and diplomatic support.
locations.directorSummary: The campaign's mobile command center. Its advanced sensors and laboratories can understand the lattice, but its damaged main deflector and limited fabrication stores prevent it from replacing regional infrastructure. Rhee's death turns every ready-room, bridge-watch, and personnel decision into part of the player's new captaincy.
locations.everydayLife: Crew move around a sealed captain's ready room, environmental specialists maintain rolling load plots in astrometrics, and the industrial replicator ledger is read at every senior briefing.

locations.title: Crown Station
locations.playerSummary: The Accord's public control center and diplomatic hub, positioned between Aurelia and Pelagos.
locations.directorSummary: Crown Station hosts traffic control, the Accord Secretariat, public telemetry, hearings, and visiting delegations. Its displays omit several categories of burden by design. It is both essential infrastructure and the stage on which continuity presents itself as transparency.
locations.everydayLife: School groups watch weather maps in the public gallery, engineers pass through access gates beneath political banners, and every delegation maintains a separate room full of private calculations.

locations.title: Aurelia
locations.playerSummary: The Accord's temperate political and agricultural center, with the system's largest population and most visible public institutions.
locations.directorSummary: Aurelia imports water vapor, exports waste heat, and benefits from the appearance that its temperate climate is the natural center of the system. Its population is not uniformly privileged: food workers, dry-belt settlements, and urban ration districts bear real risk when allocations change.
locations.everydayLife: Weather bulletins are civic ritual, market prices respond to humidity forecasts, and children are taught that every loaf of bread is a gift from five worlds.

locations.title: Solis Terraces
locations.playerSummary: A continent-scale agricultural district on Aurelia whose climate towers feed much of the Ilyra System.
locations.directorSummary: The opening surge exposes how tightly Aurelia's food security depends on imported Pelagic moisture and exported heat. Repairing the terraces without changing allocation perpetuates the old system; allowing losses creates immediate hunger and political backlash.
locations.everydayLife: Cooperative crews ride maintenance skiffs between climate towers, harvest forecasts are announced like election results, and emergency shelters double as seed banks.

locations.title: Pelagos
locations.playerSummary: An ocean world of floating cities, desalination platforms, and deep-water ecosystems that supplies water vapor and biological catalysts to the lattice.
locations.directorSummary: Pelagos considers water a sovereign contribution, not an infinite reservoir. Its official population model excludes seasonal and mobile communities, making export obligations larger than leaders admit. Reformers support Nacre while fearing Pelagos will become the next sacrifice world.
locations.everydayLife: Communities move with storm lanes, public assemblies meet on linked flotillas, and families track water-export quotas beside tide predictions.

locations.title: Caldera Array
locations.playerSummary: A deep-water desalination and vapor-transfer complex that controls a large share of Pelagos' contribution to the lattice.
locations.directorSummary: The Array can buffer exports, but doing so changes sea level and storm behavior. It stores old census assumptions in operational code. Pelagic technicians can help rewrite the model if protected from political blame.
locations.everydayLife: Divers work in rotating pressure teams, shift changes are marked by sonar chimes, and civic observers can watch every export valve from a glass control gallery.

locations.title: Viridia
locations.playerSummary: A forested world with exceptional biodiversity and a small population that supplies much of the lattice's biological processing capacity.
locations.directorSummary: Viridia acts as the system's living scrubber. Its conservation culture supports equity but rejects fixes that industrialize ecosystems or treat biodiversity as consumable infrastructure. Some conservators will sabotage projects they believe cross irreversible thresholds.
locations.everydayLife: Settlements are built around migration corridors, local weather reports include pollinator counts, and visitors are expected to justify every sealed road and cleared landing area.

locations.title: Oros Valley Reserve
locations.playerSummary: A protected Viridian valley containing rare biomes used as a reference baseline for the entire planet.
locations.directorSummary: Oros Valley can be quarantined from lattice processing, preserving ecological continuity while shifting chemical load elsewhere. It is the clearest place to demonstrate that equal tonnage is not equal harm.
locations.everydayLife: Field stations operate on minimal power, research crews travel on elevated paths, and conservation votes are held beside long-term species maps rather than in administrative halls.

locations.title: Ferrum
locations.playerSummary: A cold, metal-rich industrial world that receives heat and oxygen in exchange for components needed across the system.
locations.directorSummary: Ferrum's worker councils know the lattice makes their lives possible and gives their fabrication labor political leverage. They distrust comfortable-world environmental rhetoric that treats Ferrum machinery as emergency inventory while excluding workers from decisions.
locations.everydayLife: Shift assemblies are broadcast from factory floors, heat credits appear on household accounts, and every civic building contains an emergency oxygen room.

locations.title: Khepri City
locations.playerSummary: Ferrum's largest enclosed city and fabrication center, dependent on lattice heat and oxygen exchange.
locations.directorSummary: Khepri can manufacture processor frames, shield components, and orbital supports if the Combine commits labor. School habitats and residential sectors share heat loops with industrial lines, making any shutdown morally and politically complex.
locations.everydayLife: Residents carry compact respirators, children learn pressure-door drills, and union notices share walls with production targets and memorial plaques.

locations.title: Nacre
locations.playerSummary: A clouded world of sealed cities, corrosive rain, and expanding toxic seas whose maintenance culture keeps difficult environmental systems operating.
locations.directorSummary: Nacre has absorbed imported pollutants for generations. Its people possess the system's deepest practical maintenance knowledge and the least political power. The Nacre Assembly contains moderates, secessionists, and direct-action cells; no one group speaks for the whole world.
locations.everydayLife: Children identify rain chemistry by sound on shield roofs, public art is etched into replaceable corrosion panels, and maintenance shifts are treated as civic service.

locations.title: Harmonic Spindle
locations.playerSummary: The primary energy and atmospheric-distribution node linking Viridia, Ferrum, and Nacre.
locations.directorSummary: The Spindle can reroute heat, gases, and atmospheric mass rapidly, but every emergency adjustment creates delayed consequences. Whoever controls its authorization keys can make temporary decisions with system-wide political force.
locations.everydayLife: Multiplanet crews work in mixed teams, every control surface displays three competing safety margins, and access corridors are lined with the names of technicians killed keeping the node online.

locations.title: Umbra Manifold
locations.playerSummary: A deep-orbit complex near Nacre that compresses toxic compounds and exports stabilized material.
locations.directorSummary: Umbra contains obsolete processors, dangerous maintenance zones, original Accord records, and the best opportunity to build a genuine closed-cycle replacement. Some physical archives survived because Nacre technicians never trusted central databases.
locations.everydayLife: Crews sleep in rotating clean rooms, labels are engraved rather than printed, and maintenance apprentices memorize evacuation routes before they learn the control software.

locations.title: Ilyra Habitat Six
locations.playerSummary: A growing orbital settlement outside the five-world Accord that becomes an emergency transfer and fabrication point.
locations.directorSummary: Habitat Six began as a temporary construction camp and was excluded from the Accord's representation formulas. It can provide evacuation capacity and neutral meeting space, but recognition raises the question of whether the successor system has five members or six.
locations.everydayLife: Residents reuse construction modules as homes, communal kitchens face the system map, and every civic debate begins with whether temporary people are allowed permanent rights.

routes.title: Eudora Vale to Crown Station Approach

factions.title: Starfleet Ilyra Review Mission
factions.playerSummary: The Starfleet command responsible for the Eudora Vale's technical review, emergency response, and liaison with Federation authorities.
factions.directorSummary: Rear Admiral Osei wants no mass-casualty event, no occupation, and a settlement that survives review. Starfleet is willing to grant discretion when the player documents risks, but worries that unilateral recognition or disclosure will create precedents elsewhere.

factions.title: Ilyra Accord Secretariat
factions.playerSummary: The interworld institution that administers the terraforming lattice and represents continuity of the five-world Accord.
factions.directorSummary: The Secretariat contains competent administrators, frightened continuity advocates, and officials who knowingly maintain curated telemetry. Vorr did not create the original fraud, but he chose to preserve concealment after learning enough to understand its political purpose.

factions.title: Nacre Assembly
factions.playerSummary: Nacre's elected coalition seeking medical support, control over local nodes, public recognition of environmental harm, and a binding role in any successor system.
factions.directorSummary: The Assembly includes staged reformers, immediate secessionists, labor delegates, public-health advocates, and families who have stopped believing promises. It can cooperate if authority is real rather than consultative.

factions.title: Lattice Engineers' Union
factions.playerSummary: The cross-world union whose technicians possess the practical knowledge required to operate, repair, or redesign the lattice.
factions.directorSummary: The Union is the system's most credible cross-world institution but has been used as a politically neutral instrument. Its strike threat is both labor action and a claim that no allocation is legitimate without the people who execute it.

factions.title: Pelagic Council
factions.playerSummary: Pelagos' elected interflotilla council, focused on water sovereignty and protection against becoming the next environmental sink.
factions.directorSummary: The Council supports Nacre's claim in principle while using procedure to extract enforceable caps. It is internally divided between mobile communities, array operators, and city blocs.

factions.title: Ferrum Combine
factions.playerSummary: A federation of worker councils and industrial cooperatives capable of supplying much of the hardware needed for lattice reconstruction.
factions.directorSummary: The Combine can mobilize fabrication or a system-wide stoppage. It will cooperate when treated as a governing partner and resist when labor is requisitioned as if it were Starfleet inventory.

factions.title: Viridian Conservancy
factions.playerSummary: The scientific and civic coalition responsible for protecting Viridia's biodiversity and biological processing reserve.
factions.directorSummary: The Conservancy supports equity but rejects fixes that make Viridia a less visible sacrifice. A radical minority will damage industrial projects it believes threaten irreversible ecological loss.

factions.title: Accord Security Service
factions.playerSummary: The interworld security organization responsible for node protection, traffic control, and investigation of sabotage.
factions.directorSummary: Most officers are legitimate public-safety personnel. Director Orin and a small continuity cell stage one false-flag incident to justify emergency control. The Service can fracture, reform, or become an enforcement arm depending on how evidence is handled.

factions.title: Helioform Consortium
factions.playerSummary: A private climate-engineering consortium offering rapid orbital processors, financing, and maintenance contracts.
factions.directorSummary: Helioform can deliver real short-term capacity. Its price is long-term control of processing infrastructure, proprietary telemetry, and priority repayment clauses that favor politically stable worlds.

factions.title: Unburdened Network
factions.playerSummary: A loose Nacre direct-action network demanding immediate transfer of node control and an end to unconsented load imports.
factions.directorSummary: Some cells sabotage relays; others protect protesters, leak records, or maintain essential systems during seizures. They do not share one leader or complete system knowledge. Treating every cell as terrorist organization strengthens the most reckless wing.

actors.name: Rear Admiral Celeste Osei
actors.title: Director, Starfleet Regional Response
actors.playerSummary: The admiral overseeing the Ilyra review. She supports documented extraordinary action but has little tolerance for unilateral occupation or avoidable public chaos.
actors.directorSummary: Osei knows the review was politically sensitive and that Rhee had requested broader medical access. She does not know the original rotation or false flag at campaign start. She may offer a replacement captain if scrutiny, ship damage, or political fracture becomes severe.

knowledgeBoundary.summary: Knows Starfleet orders and Rhee's formal reports
Knows the lattice is under strain
Does not know the concealed rotation or full Nacre load

actors.name: First Minister Elian Vorr
actors.title: First Minister of the Ilyra Accord
actors.playerSummary: Aurelia's senior statesman and public face of the Accord, intelligent and convinced that continuity must survive long enough for reform to work.
actors.directorSummary: Vorr discovered four years ago that public categories hid imported burden and chose not to expose it without a transition plan. He knows concealment is morally compromised and believes sudden truth could kill millions.

knowledgeBoundary.summary: Knows the dashboards omit categories
Knows Nacre is carrying disproportionate load
Does not possess every original engineering record or understand every shutdown consequence

actors.name: Speaker Mara Senn
actors.title: Speaker of the Nacre Assembly
actors.playerSummary: A former maintenance engineer whose patience with promises is exhausted. She can support staged reform only if Nacre receives real authority and enforceable limits.
actors.directorSummary: Senn knows local telemetry, health effects, and the history of failed petitions. She does not know the complete system model and cannot guarantee control of every direct-action cell.

knowledgeBoundary.summary: Knows Nacre's maintenance data and political history
Knows some Unburdened organizers
Does not know the full immediate shutdown consequences

actors.name: Convenor Kesh Var
actors.title: Convenor, Lattice Engineers' Union
actors.playerSummary: A veteran systems engineer who insists technicians are political actors whenever they are ordered to decide who receives air, water, or heat.
actors.directorSummary: Var knows certifications were altered and that many engineers signed under pressure. He can coordinate a system-wide repair effort or strike, but will not expose members without protection and a credible accountability process.

knowledgeBoundary.summary: Knows practical load paths and altered certifications
Knows the Union's internal dissent
Does not know all Secretariat political decisions

actors.name: Councillor Rian Oso
actors.title: Senior Delegate, Pelagic Council
actors.playerSummary: A patient negotiator who supports Nacre's case but refuses open-ended water obligations or formulas based on populations Pelagos knows are undercounted.
actors.directorSummary: Oso uses delay as leverage because Pelagos has repeatedly been promised later safeguards. He will move quickly when offered enforceable caps, corrected census data, and appeal rights.

knowledgeBoundary.summary: Knows Pelagic census and water-export discrepancies
Knows Council factions
Does not know the original rotation documents

actors.name: Foreperson Dela Marr
actors.title: First Convenor, Ferrum Combine
actors.playerSummary: A worker-council leader who can mobilize fabrication capacity or a system-wide stoppage and judges outsiders by whether they treat labor as partners.
actors.directorSummary: Marr knows Ferrum leaders threatened withdrawal during the first attempted rotation and carries inherited shame about the decision. She will support truth if Ferrum is not simply assigned a new unbounded sacrifice.

knowledgeBoundary.summary: Knows Ferrum's dependency and production capacity
Knows labor history around the delayed rotation
Does not know the full dashboard redesign

actors.name: Ecologist Sorel Thann
actors.title: Senior Ecologist, Viridian Conservancy
actors.playerSummary: A systems ecologist who argues that a mathematically equal allocation can still be ecologically suicidal.
actors.directorSummary: Thann knows the planet is near a non-linear threshold and suspects a radical cell has damaged an industrial test site. He will not conceal ecological danger to preserve an appearance of unity.

knowledgeBoundary.summary: Knows Viridian threshold data
Knows Conservancy factions
Does not know the complete Nacre burden history

actors.name: Director Cael Orin
actors.title: Director, Accord Security Service
actors.playerSummary: The professional head of Accord Security, requesting Starfleet support against sabotage while insisting local civil authority remain intact.
actors.directorSummary: Orin authorizes one false-flag relay attack through a small continuity cell to justify node-control powers. He believes temporary coercion will prevent a real shutdown. Most of the Service does not know.

knowledgeBoundary.summary: Knows security investigations and the false-flag cell
Knows some Secretariat omissions
Does not know the exact original rotation or all Unburdened cells

actors.name: Doctor Lyra Veen
actors.title: Director, Nacre Public Health Registry
actors.playerSummary: A public-health physician maintaining incomplete but devastating records of chronic illness across Nacre's sealed cities.
actors.directorSummary: Veen can establish long-term harm and mortality but has protected some patient data from both the Secretariat and militants. She will share under consent and custody safeguards.

knowledgeBoundary.summary: Knows patient patterns and local exposure
Knows registry gaps
Does not know every source allocation or political decision

actors.name: Avar Jess
actors.title: Nodekeeper and Unburdened Organizer
actors.playerSummary: A Nacre nodekeeper associated with direct-action organizers who argue that continued unconsented operation is itself violence.
actors.directorSummary: Jess helps seize a node but continues essential services. Another cell, not Jess, destroys a relay. Jess has an incomplete system model and can be persuaded by credible shared authority.

knowledgeBoundary.summary: Knows Nacre node procedures and some direct-action plans
Does not control every cell
Does not know full shutdown consequences

actors.name: Alix Meral
actors.title: Special Projects Director, Helioform Consortium
actors.playerSummary: A persuasive climate engineer offering rapid orbital processors, financing, and a complete maintenance package.
actors.directorSummary: Meral's technology works, though not at the scale claimed without local components. The contract would create long-term control and data asymmetry. She is not sabotaging public alternatives; she benefits from their delay.

knowledgeBoundary.summary: Knows consortium performance limits and contract terms
Knows public telemetry
Does not know hidden archives or false flag

actors.name: Coordinator Nia Tess
actors.title: Civic Coordinator, Ilyra Habitat Six
actors.playerSummary: The elected coordinator of an orbital settlement excluded from the Accord despite becoming essential to construction and evacuation.
actors.directorSummary: Tess wants representation, not charity. Habitat Six can serve as neutral ground and evacuation capacity if recognized. It also contains undocumented workers who fear census exposure.

knowledgeBoundary.summary: Knows habitat capacity and labor networks
Knows who the Accord excludes
Does not know the concealed rotation

actors.name: Ines Quill
actors.title: Retired Lattice Auditor
actors.playerSummary: A retired auditor who remained near Umbra Manifold after leaving the Secretariat under disputed circumstances.
actors.directorSummary: Quill preserved physical copies of the original rotation clause and knows how public categories were redesigned. She participated in the redesign under threat of system withdrawal and has waited for an authority she believes can protect the record.

knowledgeBoundary.summary: Knows original rotation and dashboard redesign
Knows some names in the certification chain
Does not know current sabotage plans

actors.name: Minister Talia Iven
actors.title: Aurelian Minister for Food and Climate
actors.playerSummary: The official responsible for food security on Aurelia, under intense pressure to prevent crop loss and public panic.
actors.directorSummary: Iven knows Aurelia receives more moisture than public civic education implies. She does not know the full Nacre burden and will support transparent rationing if presented with credible system-wide support.

knowledgeBoundary.summary: Knows Aurelia allocation and crop windows
Knows public sentiment
Does not know original rotation or security false flag

campaign.title: Broken Accord
campaign.highConcept:
The Ilyra System survives because five worlds share one impossible machine: a terraforming lattice that moves heat, water, air, and biological debt through every treaty, harvest, and childhood. Then a surge kills Captain Nasrin Rhee, fractures the public model, and exposes the first shape of an old lie. One world has been paying for the others in ways the Accord was never allowed to name.

Into that concealed failure steps the U.S.S. Eudora Vale: fast, capable, grieving, and suddenly under your independent command. The ship can model the lattice, bridge a failing node, and keep a city alive for one more day. It cannot manufacture legitimacy, undo generations of hidden sacrifice, or make five governments tell the truth before the next cascade begins.

Every intervention chooses who breathes easier and who carries the cost. Workers, ministers, engineers, assemblies, and terrified families all need Starfleet power while fearing what Starfleet proof will unleash. To keep Ilyra alive, you will need to command the ship, expose the burden, and decide whether a broken accord can be repaired without breaking the worlds it held together.
campaign.thesis: A common system is not just because its benefits are shared; its burdens must be visible, consented to, revisable, and governed by those who carry them.
startingDirectives: Protect civilian life and viable futures across the Ilyra System.
Keep the terraforming lattice stable while avoiding hidden, unconsented burdens.
Preserve public evidence, independent telemetry, and lawful review of allocation decisions.
Respect local authority, technical conscience, and Nacre representation.
Maintain Starfleet authority as emergency support, not permanent civil ownership.

endingAxes.title: System Survival
endingAxes.summary: How many worlds retain viable populations, infrastructure, and safe transition paths after the cascade.

endingAxes.title: Burden Justice
endingAxes.summary: Whether Nacre's involuntary role ends and future costs are visible, bounded, revisable, and consented to.

endingAxes.title: Political Legitimacy
endingAxes.summary: Whether the body issuing final orders is recognized by those expected to obey them.

endingAxes.title: Ecological Continuity
endingAxes.summary: Whether Aurelia, Pelagos, Viridia, Ferrum, and Nacre preserve long-term habitability rather than only immediate survival.

endingAxes.title: Starfleet Role
endingAxes.summary: Whether Starfleet acts as witness, guarantor, emergency trustee, coercive allocator, or departing partner.

endingAxes.title: Captaincy Outcome
endingAxes.summary: Whether the player is confirmed, retained under inquiry, censured, relieved, or chooses transition after the crisis.

endingProfiles: The finale is not unlocked by a chapter cursor. It requires the successor-authority quest, an interim allocation history, and either a redundancy disposition or sufficiently advanced cascade pressure.
The Director selects only crisis fronts that remain causally active after prior repairs, side assignments, alliances, and sacrifices.
An earned asset must correspond to an actor, institution, route, technical capability, or documented agreement that can actually act during the finale.
No ending family is a hidden perfect answer. Each is derived from system survival, burden justice, legitimacy, ecology, Starfleet posture, and command conduct.
The epilogue records continuing obligations and command review rather than resetting the Ilyra System to a generic stable state.

bands.label: A Common Climate
bands.summary: All five populations retain viable futures, Nacre is no longer a permanent unconsented sink, and shared authority becomes enforceable and public.

bands.label: Reformed Accord
bands.summary: The system survives under a legitimate transition with hard limits, review, telemetry, and a path toward fairer burden-sharing.

bands.label: Five Separate Skies
bands.summary: The shared authority dissolves or narrows, but warning, navigation, aid, and controlled separation preserve viable futures.

bands.label: Peace By Allocation
bands.summary: Emergency technical control limits casualties, but consent, representation, or durable legitimacy remains badly compromised.

bands.label: The Fifth World Falls
bands.summary: The other worlds survive while Nacre suffers collapse, mass evacuation, or an unrepaired sacrifice.

bands.label: Broken Worlds
bands.summary: The branch ends in multi-world catastrophe unless the player replays or pushes into a radically changed aftermath premise.

continuationFrames.title: Court-Martial And Inquiry
continuationFrames.summary: Play continues through investigation, testimony, public evidence disputes, and emergency-relief obligations under formal constraint.
continuationFrames.authorityModel: The player has constrained Starfleet authority, but remains a material witness and can still shape relief, records, and accountability.
continuationFrames.playableRole: Constrained commander or material witness during inquiry.
requiredPlausibilitySignals: Surviving records, a lawful hearing authority, and unresolved Ilyra relief obligations.
promptContextAdjustments: Frame future scenes around hearing limits, testimony, technical evidence, and continuing relief obligations.
continuationFrames.playerFacingStartCopy: The next scene begins under inquiry. Your authority is constrained, but your record and testimony can still affect Ilyra.

continuationFrames.title: Relieved But Advising
continuationFrames.summary: A replacement commander holds the chair while the player remains an expert adviser on the lattice crisis.
continuationFrames.authorityModel: The player cannot issue unrestricted orders, but senior staff and local authorities may still seek their technical and moral judgment.
continuationFrames.playableRole: Relieved captain serving as technical and diplomatic adviser.
requiredPlausibilitySignals: A replacement command chain, surviving crew trust, and unresolved lattice choices.
promptContextAdjustments: Keep command limits explicit and make advice consequential through staff, local delegates, and technical records.
continuationFrames.playerFacingStartCopy: A new commander has the chair. You remain close enough to the crisis for your advice to matter.

continuationFrames.title: Survivors After Eudora Vale Loss
continuationFrames.summary: The ship is lost, but survivors continue from emergency cells, allied facilities, or local command centers.
continuationFrames.authorityModel: The player can coordinate survivors and relief partners if communication, staff continuity, and local recognition remain plausible.
continuationFrames.playableRole: Survivor command lead or relief coordinator after ship loss.
requiredPlausibilitySignals: Surviving senior staff, a local base, and at least one unresolved civilian-protection objective.
promptContextAdjustments: The Eudora Vale remains lost. Future scenes use survivor resources, allied logistics, and local bases.
continuationFrames.playerFacingStartCopy: The Eudora Vale is gone. The survivors still have people to save and decisions to make.

continuationFrames.title: Allied System Command Frame
continuationFrames.summary: Play continues from an allied vessel, Crown Station, or a lawful emergency council after Eudora Vale command is compromised.
continuationFrames.authorityModel: The player operates by compact, relief authority, or delegated Starfleet mandate rather than sole ship command.
continuationFrames.playableRole: Emergency liaison or delegated command partner.
requiredPlausibilitySignals: An allied platform, lawful recognition, and unresolved system stabilization work.
promptContextAdjustments: Emphasize coalition command, limited resources, and formal recognition by affected Ilyra authorities.
continuationFrames.playerFacingStartCopy: Your command has moved into an allied frame. You still have authority, but it now depends on the coalition holding together.

continuationFrames.title: Aftermath Relief And Allocation
continuationFrames.summary: The central settlement has failed or narrowed, but relief, migration, evidence preservation, and reparations remain playable.
continuationFrames.authorityModel: The player cannot undo the failed settlement, but can still choose who receives aid, whose evidence is preserved, and what obligations endure.
requiredPlausibilitySignals: At least one surviving population, active relief channels, and unresolved accountability or migration choices.
promptContextAdjustments: Shift goals from preventing collapse to triage, migration, reparations, public record, and long-term obligations.
continuationFrames.playerFacingStartCopy: The settlement has broken. The next choices are about relief, truth, and who is not abandoned.

continuationFrames.title: Medical Survival And Command Gap
continuationFrames.summary: A lethal outcome becomes recovery, incapacity, or disputed survival while the command gap creates new pressure.
continuationFrames.authorityModel: The player is alive but their authority is interrupted until medical, legal, or command continuity questions resolve.
continuationFrames.playableRole: Recovering commander or temporarily incapacitated captain.
requiredPlausibilitySignals: Medical plausibility, surviving staff, and a reason the player can regain agency.
promptContextAdjustments: Treat recovery as consequential: time passes, others act, and command confidence may change.
continuationFrames.playerFacingStartCopy: You survived, but command did not pause for you. Recovery and succession are now part of the crisis.

continuationFrames.title: Retired But Testifying
continuationFrames.summary: The player leaves active command but continues through testimony, debriefing, public record, and remaining obligations.
continuationFrames.authorityModel: The player no longer commands the Eudora Vale, but their testimony and preserved evidence still shape the settlement.
requiredPlausibilitySignals: Accepted resignation or transfer, preserved records, and an unresolved inquiry or settlement process.
promptContextAdjustments: Move play into testimony, final obligations, and what the player chooses to put on the record.
continuationFrames.playerFacingStartCopy: Your command is over. Your record, testimony, and remaining promises are not.

templates.title: The Captain's Chair
templates.summary: A lattice surge tears open a Crown Station habitat during the Eudora Vale's approach. Captain Nasrin Rhee leads a shuttle evacuation while the XO coordinates the ship, and a secondary inversion kills Rhee after the evacuees transport clear, forcing the player to assume command and choose where the system will carry the emergency load.
templates.playerSummary: A lattice surge tears open a Crown Station habitat during the Eudora Vale's approach. Captain Nasrin Rhee leads a shuttle evacuation while the XO coordinates the ship, and a secondary inversion kills Rhee after the evacuees transport clear, forcing the player to assume command and choose where the system will carry the emergency load.
templates.directorSummary: The prelude establishes ordinary XO competence, Rhee's death, lawful succession, damaged deflector capacity, the first persistent allocation consequence, and the campaign rule that every rescue shifts pressure somewhere else. The cause is genuinely uncertain at first; do not force sabotage as the opening answer.
templates.dramaticQuestion: What kind of captain does the player become when their first command decision must make one population carry a cost?

objectives.summary: Assume principal mission command during the Crown Station approach and review the ship's lattice-response limits.
Rescue the maintenance-habitat population and prevent Crown Station from losing orbital control.
Coordinate Rhee's shuttle evacuation and the Eudora Vale transport response.
Commit an emergency load-shedding or ship-interposition plan with incomplete telemetry.
Assume lawful command after Rhee's death, appoint or define an acting XO arrangement, and issue the first standing orders.

pressures.summary: Crown Station gravity and life support are failing in different sections.
The Eudora Vale main deflector can synchronize only briefly before overload.
Public telemetry does not agree with raw maintenance readings.
Every safe load-shedding route creates weather, health, industrial, or ecological consequences elsewhere.
The crew must process Rhee's death while the system's governments demand an explanation.

revelations.summary: The surge is distributed through several nodes rather than confined to Crown Station.
Rhee's shuttle passengers transport clear before a secondary power inversion destroys the craft.
Raw node readings contain categories absent from Crown Station's public displays.

approaches: Use the Eudora Vale deflector and structural systems to absorb part of the surge.
Concentrate the emergency load on one world or infrastructure node.
Distribute smaller harms across several locations with local consent where time allows.
Delegate portions of the rescue to local traffic control, union crews, and shuttles.
Challenge the official allocation recommendation and improvise a different causal solution.

outcomes.summary: The player distributes the emergency load across several locations and accepts visible but limited harm on multiple worlds.

effects.summary: Emergency allocations propagate across the five-world lattice rather than remaining local.

outcomes.summary: The player saves Crown Station by concentrating the burden on one chosen world or node, creating a persistent local crisis.

templates.title: Bread and Weather
templates.summary: Solis Terraces loses humidity control and begins shedding heat into an already unstable lattice. Aurelia asks the Eudora Vale to restore the crop climate before a harvest window closes, while Pelagos warns that the requested moisture draw will threaten floating settlements.
templates.playerSummary: Solis Terraces loses humidity control and begins shedding heat into an already unstable lattice. Aurelia asks the Eudora Vale to restore the crop climate before a harvest window closes, while Pelagos warns that the requested moisture draw will threaten floating settlements.
templates.directorSummary: This quest makes Aurelia sympathetic and privileged at the same time. It should reveal real food risk, the world's imported moisture dependence, and the political habit of treating Aurelian stability as system stability. The player can save crops without preserving every existing allocation.
templates.dramaticQuestion: How much immediate food security may one world demand from a system whose old guarantees concealed their cost?

objectives.summary: Stabilize the climate towers and protect workers, seed vaults, and food stores.
Determine how much moisture and heat exchange the terraces actually require.
Coordinate or contest Aurelia's request for priority lattice access.
Account for consequences to Pelagos, Ferrum, and the ship's fabrication reserves.
Establish whether local and public telemetry will record the full intervention.

pressures.summary: The crop window is measured in hours, not political cycles.
A full moisture draw raises Pelagic sea-level risk.
Aurelia's population treats food protection as an obvious system priority.
Industrial replicator feedstock can fabricate climate components or be saved for later system repairs.

revelations.summary: Aurelia imports substantially more Pelagic water vapor than civic summaries disclose.
Solis Terraces export waste heat that Ferrum and the Spindle use as a predictable baseline.
The current control logic gives Aurelian crop stability priority during ambiguous states.

approaches: Restore full climate control through a large Pelagic moisture draw.
Ration the terraces and preserve seed and staple crops rather than the full harvest.
Use the Eudora Vale and local weather towers for a temporary closed atmospheric cycle.
Negotiate a transparent emergency exchange with Pelagos and Ferrum.
Accept crop losses while redirecting reserves toward a system-wide alternative.

outcomes.summary: The harvest is largely preserved through priority moisture and heat allocation, increasing stability while moving cost elsewhere.

templates.playerSummary: Pelagos suffers rapid sea-level oscillation around the Caldera Array as export demands continue to use an obsolete census. Floating communities omitted from the official model are now inside the projected storm-surge zone.
templates.directorSummary: This quest establishes Pelagos as both donor and vulnerable society. The census discrepancy is not a clerical twist; it reflects mobile communities repeatedly excluded from fixed-world representation. Water sovereignty can become an asset or a wedge.

objectives.summary: Protect the Caldera Array and floating communities from rapid sea-level change.

templates.title: Borrowed Breath
templates.summary: A heat-exchange failure at Khepri City forces Ferrum to choose between residential life support and fabrication lines needed across the system. Worker councils refuse to let Crown Station make the choice through an automated priority table.
templates.playerSummary: A heat-exchange failure at Khepri City forces Ferrum to choose between residential life support and fabrication lines needed across the system. Worker councils refuse to let Crown Station make the choice through an automated priority table.
templates.directorSummary: Ferrum should feel neither selfish nor reducible to a factory. The quest reveals its existential dependence, industrial leverage, and labor governance. The player can earn fabrication support only by treating workers as political partners.
templates.dramaticQuestion: When survival and production share the same heat loop, who has authority to decide which one counts as essential?

objectives.summary: Prevent fatal heat and oxygen loss in Khepri City.

approaches: Prioritize habitats and suspend fabrication.

templates.title: The Green Lung

templates.playerSummary: A delayed chemical pulse reaches Viridia and begins altering the Oros Valley reserve. The Conservancy asks Starfleet to quarantine the valley from lattice processing, while Crown Station warns that the unprocessed compounds must be sent somewhere else.
templates.directorSummary: This quest establishes ecology as a full ending axis. It should make clear that equal mass does not produce equal harm, and that Viridian resistance can be principled without being consequence-free.
templates.dramaticQuestion: Can a living world be treated as infrastructure without reducing it to a consumable machine?

objectives.summary: Measure and contain the chemical pulse before it crosses ecological thresholds.
Protect researchers, settlements, and critical species corridors.
Decide whether to quarantine Oros Valley from lattice processing.

pressures.summary: Biological effects may become irreversible before political authorization arrives.

revelations.summary: Viridia's biosphere processes compounds exported by every other world.
Oros Valley is close to a non-linear ecological threshold that tonnage-based models do not represent.

templates.directorSummary: This is a command and public-legitimacy quest, not a binary publish/suppress choice. The player can sequence disclosure, share custody, set a timetable, protect vulnerable infrastructure, or let local institutions lead. Every route should clearly state foreseeable risks.
templates.dramaticQuestion: Can truth be staged without becoming another form of control?

objectives.summary: Assess immediate infrastructure, health, and public-order risks of disclosure.

pressures.summary: Nacre organizers believe delay proves Starfleet has joined the concealment.

revelations.summary: Immediate lattice shutdown would cause severe harm on every world, including Nacre.

No disclosure sequence prevents all panic, coercion, or infrastructure risk.

approaches: Publish the complete evidence immediately with a Starfleet technical warning.
Set a short public timetable tied to emergency safeguards and independent oversight.

Refuse Starfleet control and let Nacre or the Union publish under their own authority.
Maintain temporary confidentiality while building redundancy, accepting the legitimacy cost.

templates.title: Rain Country
templates.summary: A corrosive storm exceeds the shield margins of a Nacre city while hospitals report a surge in chronic exposure cases. The Nacre Assembly will cooperate with Starfleet relief only if the mission treats local records and maintenance crews as authorities rather than evidence sources to be extracted.
templates.playerSummary: A corrosive storm exceeds the shield margins of a Nacre city while hospitals report a surge in chronic exposure cases. The Nacre Assembly will cooperate with Starfleet relief only if the mission treats local records and maintenance crews as authorities rather than evidence sources to be extracted.
templates.directorSummary: This quest turns Nacre into a full recurring hub and grounds the hidden burden in ordinary life. It should offer rescue, medicine, engineering, diplomacy, and crew interaction without making the world only a site of suffering.
templates.dramaticQuestion: What does recognition look like before a treaty exists, when local competence is already keeping people alive?

objectives.summary: Protect the sealed city and maintain shield coverage through the corrosive storm.
Expand medical care without seizing patient records or overriding consent.
Establish direct working authority with Nacre maintenance crews and the Assembly.
Identify which imported loads are driving the current storm chemistry.
Create a sustainable return route for future Nacre assignments.

pressures.summary: Shield emitters and medical fabrication require the same limited feedstock.
Patients fear their records will be used to justify evacuation or political claims without consent.
Direct-action organizers distrust Starfleet security teams near local nodes.
The storm is partly the delayed consequence of earlier allocations.

revelations.summary: Nacre's chronic illness patterns follow imported contaminant categories rather than local geology.
Nacre nodekeepers have kept system-critical processes operating with incomplete support for decades.
The official medical registry substantially understates long-term harm.

approaches: Fabricate shield components and defer other system repairs.
Use ship systems to reinforce the city while local crews perform structural work.
Prioritize hospital capacity and evacuate the most vulnerable blocks.
Negotiate patient-controlled registry sharing and direct Assembly coordination.
Redirect storm chemistry through Umbra or the Spindle with consequences elsewhere.

outcomes.summary: Starfleet relief is conducted under a formal partnership with the Assembly, local nodekeepers, and patient-controlled data safeguards.

templates.summary: Nacre nodekeepers seize local control of a lattice junction but continue essential operations. The Secretariat calls it sabotage, the Assembly calls it emergency self-government, and the player must decide what authority Starfleet recognizes while the node remains indispensable.

templates.directorSummary: The seized node is not a hostage device. The occupiers are competent and preserve life support. The conflict is about lawful control, practical legitimacy, and whether Starfleet treats Nacre as a coequal government before a final charter exists.

objectives.summary: Prevent interruption of essential lattice functions at the seized node.

templates.title: Many Hands
templates.summary: Several damaging incidents occur across the system: a Nacre relay is sabotaged, a Security convoy is attacked, a Viridian industrial test fails, and an old Spindle component ruptures. Each faction presents a single explanation that serves its immediate interests.
templates.playerSummary: Several damaging incidents occur across the system: a Nacre relay is sabotaged, a Security convoy is attacked, a Viridian industrial test fails, and an old Spindle component ruptures. Each faction presents a single explanation that serves its immediate interests.
templates.directorSummary: This quest prevents the campaign from collapsing into a mastermind plot. At least four causal chains exist: a direct-action relay attack, a Security false flag, Viridian sabotage of an industrial proposal, and genuine material failure. The player may prove some but not all.
templates.dramaticQuestion: Can justice remain specific when every institution benefits from calling all damage the work of one enemy?

objectives.summary: Stabilize active damage before assigning blame.
Preserve separate evidence chains for each incident.
Distinguish deliberate sabotage, false attribution, reckless direct action, and ordinary failure.
Protect witnesses and technical investigators from faction pressure.
Set a public accountability policy that does not erase political context.

pressures.summary: Local governments demand immediate attribution before evidence is complete.
Some perpetrators are also essential technicians or legitimate public officials.
A broad security crackdown may prevent one attack while causing several others.
The Security false flag is protected by compartmented records and lawful-seeming orders.

revelations.summary: The incidents have multiple unrelated and partially related causes.
A small Accord Security cell staged one attack to justify emergency node authority.
Viridian radicals damaged an industrial test they believed would permanently simplify the biosphere.
At least one major failure is ordinary aging infrastructure rather than sabotage.

approaches: Publish differentiated findings and prosecute specific acts under appropriate law.
Offer conditional amnesty in exchange for evidence, repair, and disarmament.
Use one emergency security framework for all incidents.
Expose the false flag while allowing local institutions to handle other cases.
Prioritize repair and defer prosecution under preserved evidence.

outcomes.summary: The investigation publishes differentiated causes and builds a credible, specific accountability record.

templates.title: Terms of Survival
templates.summary: The system needs an interim allocation regime before permanent replacements can be built. Every credible plan requires visible sacrifices: rotating limited burdens, system-wide consumption cuts, controlled damage elsewhere, migration, or temporary technical control.
templates.playerSummary: The system needs an interim allocation regime before permanent replacements can be built. Every credible plan requires visible sacrifices: rotating limited burdens, system-wide consumption cuts, controlled damage elsewhere, migration, or temporary technical control.
templates.directorSummary: This is a portfolio-design quest rather than a single summit. The player should visit or communicate with multiple worlds, combine assets, and state a clear decision rule for future emergencies. A technically good plan without consent remains fragile; a fair plan without capacity may fail.
templates.dramaticQuestion: What temporary inequality can be justified when the alternative is immediate collapse, and who gets to define temporary?

objectives.summary: Build at least two technically viable interim allocation models.
Secure participation or document refusal from each world authority.
Define caps, review intervals, exit mechanisms, and emergency override rules.
Account for population health and ecological thresholds, not only system tonnage.
Commit an interim regime that the Director can apply to later world reactions.

pressures.summary: Every world can identify a plausible reason it cannot carry more burden now.
The next major surge may occur before full consent is reached.
A rotation that is formally equal may be medically or ecologically unequal.
Consumption cuts fall unevenly across food, housing, industry, and health.

revelations.summary: No available interim regime can eliminate all unequal burden before redundancy exists.
Time limits, health caps, public telemetry, and exit mechanisms materially change whether unequal terms remain legitimate.
Uncoordinated local vetoes can recreate collapse through cumulative refusal.

approaches: Adopt capped rotating burdens with public health and ecological limits.
Impose system-wide austerity and reduce total lattice demand.
Use technical optimization under local veto and public telemetry.
Decentralize quotas and accept slower, less efficient cooperation.
Combine migration, orbital processing, and temporary controlled harm.

outcomes.summary: A capped, time-limited rotation distributes temporary burden under public health and ecological safeguards.

effects.title: Capped Rotation Regime
effects.playerSummary: A documented interim allocation with caps, review dates, and exit mechanisms guides later emergencies.

outcomes.summary: All worlds reduce demand through rationing, production cuts, and climate concessions, preserving equity at economic and political cost.

effects.title: System Austerity Regime

templates.title: Closed Cycle
templates.summary: Umbra Manifold and the Harmonic Spindle can support a redundant orbital processing network, but only if enough fabrication, catalysts, water buffers, maintenance access, and command coordination have been earned. Helioform offers a faster proprietary alternative.
templates.playerSummary: Umbra Manifold and the Harmonic Spindle can support a redundant orbital processing network, but only if enough fabrication, catalysts, water buffers, maintenance access, and command coordination have been earned. Helioform offers a faster proprietary alternative.
templates.directorSummary: This quest reads campaign assets rather than assuming them. Missing assets do not block play; they change scale, risk, cost, and who controls the resulting infrastructure. The player can build a public network, lease a private one, construct partial redundancy, or abandon the attempt.
templates.dramaticQuestion: Can infrastructure become common property before the crisis forces someone else to own it?

objectives.summary: Select a processor architecture and ownership model.
Assemble fabrication, biological, water, and maintenance inputs.
Stabilize Umbra and Spindle integration without creating a new uncontrolled load.
Set telemetry, maintenance, and emergency-access rules.
Test the network under a real or simulated surge.

pressures.summary: The Eudora Vale cannot fabricate the network alone.
Helioform can shorten the timeline in exchange for proprietary control.
An incomplete network may shift rather than eliminate burden.
High-power synchronization risks the already damaged main deflector.

revelations.summary: A genuine closed-cycle reduction is technically possible at limited scale using Umbra and Spindle infrastructure.
No Starfleet-only build can reach useful scale before the next cascade.
Helioform's rapid system would create proprietary control over telemetry and maintenance.

approaches: Build a cooperative public network from earned local assets.
Lease Helioform processors under negotiated safeguards.
Construct partial redundancy targeted at the most dangerous loads.
Use the Eudora Vale deflector as a temporary active processor despite damage risk.
Abandon the network and invest remaining resources in evacuation and local systems.

objectives.summary: Maintain minimum life-support and surge response during the labor action.

Negotiate minimum-safety work while the strike continues.
Use Starfleet emergency requisition with explicit limits and review.
Support the strike and redirect the Eudora Vale toward emergency coverage.
Train replacement teams and accept a slower, riskier system.

templates.title: Who Was Not in the Room
templates.summary: Venn Talar keeps identifying whose consent is being represented by institutions that call their own agreement consensus.

surfacing.storyQuestion: When does a workable meeting become illegitimate because the absent were expected to carry the result?
surfacing.naturalTrigger: A summit, staff briefing, closed technical session, crew grievance, or declaration of consensus.

templates.title: The Shared Deflector
templates.summary: Every major lattice synchronization spends the Eudora Vale's deflector margin, coupler life, and future ability to intervene.

surfacing.storyQuestion: When should the ship risk losing future response capacity to prevent immediate planetary harm?
surfacing.naturalTrigger: High-energy synchronization, a deferred repair, deflector overheating, or a request to use the ship as temporary lattice infrastructure.

templates.title: The Feedstock Ledger
templates.summary: The industrial replicator can fabricate climate hardware, shield components, habitat parts, or ship repairs, but not all of them in time.

surfacing.storyQuestion: Which future capability is command willing to spend for the crisis in front of the ship?
surfacing.naturalTrigger: A major fabrication request, emergency requisition, denied repair, or side assignment that consumes a named reserve.

templates.title: The Memorial Watch
templates.summary: The crew needs recurring, nonperformative ways to mark Rhee's death while maintaining duty through a campaign that offers little quiet time.

surfacing.storyQuestion: What can the crew preserve about Rhee without making grief another readiness metric?
surfacing.naturalTrigger: A watch change, memorial request, returned personal item, anniversary, or crew member declining a ritual.

templates.title: Weather on Deck
templates.summary: Crew members begin recreating rain, tides, forest air, and dry heat in holodeck or mess-hall rituals as the planetary crisis enters shipboard life.

surfacing.storyQuestion: How does a crew remain a community while its work is measured in planetary weather?
surfacing.naturalTrigger: A meal, holodeck request, cultural observance, off-duty scene, or argument about environmental simulation power.

templates.title: The Replacement Captain
templates.summary: Starfleet may prepare a permanent replacement while the player is already carrying operational command and regional relationships.

surfacing.storyQuestion: What should Starfleet recognize as command qualification when the crisis itself is still defining the job?
surfacing.naturalTrigger: A command review, severe ship damage, public controversy, high scrutiny, or an admiral requesting a private conference.

templates.title: Ordinary Life After Succession
templates.summary: Routine competence, friendships, recreation, mentoring, and small personal obligations continue without every scene becoming a referendum on the campaign crisis.

surfacing.storyQuestion: What ordinary concern matters because the crew must live aboard the ship between emergencies?
surfacing.naturalTrigger: Observable evidence from recent play: a missed meal, hobby, promise, fatigue cue, cultural need, professional confidence issue, or recurring interest.

effects.summary: Captain Nasrin Rhee died after the Crown Station evacuation completed.
