Linked node failures and contradictory weather reports become more frequent.
Repairs and disciplined load management create longer safe planning windows.
Visible inequality increases Nacre mobilization and public resistance to shared orders.
Communities increasingly accept temporary burdens because limits, review, and reciprocity are visible.
Technicians, local governments, and civilians increasingly demand proof of authority before obeying system-wide orders.
Even unpopular allocation decisions are more likely to be implemented because the process is visible and participatory.
Nacre nodekeepers begin implementing unilateral safety limits and preparing political separation.
Industrial feedstock and replacement hardware are rationed; every fabrication request now displaces another repair.
Senior staff begin requesting explicit command boundaries, review, and relief planning.
The senior staff increasingly treats the player as the ship's captain rather than a temporary emergency holder.
Starfleet expands reporting requirements and begins considering a permanent command intervention.
Delayed ecological effects begin arriving faster than emergency engineering can treat them as isolated events.
The Lattice Engineers' Union begins a protected system-wide stoppage outside life-safety work.
Rear Admiral Osei has a permanent replacement captain available and requests a formal command conference.
A major lattice surge links at least two unresolved locations and forces immediate operational triage.

cards.title: Campaign Promise
cards.content: The player begins as the established Commander/XO of the U.S.S. Eudora Vale and becomes Acting Captain after Captain Nasrin Rhee dies in a lattice surge. Play centers on five interdependent worlds, finite ship resources, contested evidence, political legitimacy, and the consequences of deciding who carries environmental cost.

cards.title: Campaign Thesis
cards.content: A common system is not just because its benefits are shared. Burdens must be visible, consented to, bounded, revisable, and governed by those who carry them.

cards.title: Persistent Ilyra System
cards.content: The Ilyra System continues outside the foreground quest. Worlds make unilateral allocations, engineers mobilize, weather fronts advance, fabrication stores decline, and political actors respond to information and access. Do not freeze unrelated locations while the player travels.

cards.title: Every Crisis Has a Parent
cards.content: Each failure must arise from established load, aging hardware, delayed consequences, specific sabotage, institutional orders, elapsed time, or a committed decision. The campaign truth explicitly rejects one mastermind behind every event.

cards.title: Situation on Rails, Solution off Rails
cards.content: Hold fixed the opening succession, the linked lattice, the original rotation, the hidden burden, and the lethality of abrupt shutdown. Never require the player to follow a prewritten scene order or choose from listed approaches.

cards.title: Senior-Staff Briefing Format
cards.content: Briefings distinguish known facts, inference, unknowns, immediate risk, capacity, recommendations, and the command decision required. One or two officers should carry the central disagreement; avoid a round-robin moral debate.

cards.title: Eudora Vale Capability and Limits
cards.content: The Intrepid-class Eudora Vale has strong sensors, laboratories, transporters, shuttles, diplomacy spaces, and a capable deflector. It cannot replace five worlds of climate infrastructure, fabricate unlimited processors, or synchronize repeatedly without damage.

cards.title: Finale Assembly
cards.content: A Common Climate reads actual repairs, allocation commitments, successor authority, assets, route access, crew readiness, ship damage, public legitimacy, and unresolved fronts. Present only pressures that remain causally active.

cards.title: Calm Content Remains Calm
cards.content: Rhee's Quarters, A Forest Under Glass, Weather on Deck, and other ordinary or reflective material need not become sabotage scenes. Preserve quiet assignments unless current state causally brings danger to them.

cards.title: Failure-Forward Campaign
cards.content: Failed checks create cost, delay, exposure, lost leverage, injury, damaged trust, narrower options, or transformed objectives. Critical truths have alternate routes and severe outcomes continue through evacuation, inquiry, and recovery.

cards.title: The Original Rotation
cards.content: The original Ilyra Accord required the environmental sink role to rotate every twelve years. Nacre was the first sink and should not have remained one permanently.

cards.title: Why Rotation Was Deferred
cards.content: The first rotation was deferred after an Aurelian crop collapse and a Ferrum withdrawal threat. What began as emergency delay became permanent policy because every later government inherited the benefits.

cards.title: The Curated Dashboard
cards.content: Public categories were redesigned so imported heat, chemical burden, atmospheric waste, and storm energy arriving on Nacre appeared to be local geology and weather.

cards.title: Abrupt Shutdown Is Lethal
cards.content: Immediate disconnection would cause crop loss, sea-level instability, heat failure, toxic atmospheric accumulation, and mass displacement across all five worlds. The injustice is real, and so is dependency.

cards.title: Sabotage Has Multiple Authors
cards.content: Nacre direct-action cells, Accord Security false-flag operators, Viridian extremists, opportunistic contractors, and ordinary material failures each cause distinct incidents. Preserve separate evidence chains.

cards.title: Security False Flag
cards.content: A limited Accord Security team stages one attack to justify centralized Spindle control. Director Cael Orin does not begin knowing every detail; do not make the entire service complicit without evidence.

cards.title: Rhee's Delayed Review
cards.content: Rhee suspected omitted categories and accepted a delayed public review in exchange for direct medical access to Nacre and a broader independent audit. This was a compromise under uncertainty, not a final solution or secret instruction.

cards.title: Fenn's Prior Certification
cards.content: Milo Fenn helped certify a modernization six years earlier using curated categories. He did not knowingly falsify data, but his signature is relevant and his later candor affects crew and public trust.

cards.title: Assume the Chair
cards.content: After Rhee's death, the player must issue lawful standing orders, define the Acting XO arrangement, and make the command transition visible without narrating the player's private grief.

cards.title: The First Sacrifice
cards.content: The opening load shed must name the population, system, or ship capability carrying the cost. Do not hide it in generic technical success.

cards.title: Publish the Model
cards.content: When raw telemetry can be released, pause on timing, custody, explanation, and transition capacity rather than reducing disclosure to honest versus dishonest.

cards.title: Patients Are Not Evidence
cards.content: During Nacre medical work, command must decide how health data supports public truth without treating patients as involuntary evidence repositories.

cards.title: Recognize or Retake
cards.content: A Nacre node seizure is simultaneously essential service, political self-government, treaty breach, and potential safety risk. Force is not the default answer.

cards.title: Refuse the Single Villain
cards.content: When factions demand one culprit, command may insist on incident-specific findings even when a simpler public story would be useful.

cards.title: Sign the Interim Allocation
cards.content: Any temporary allocation must identify review dates, safety limits, affected populations, enforcement authority, and a path to exit or revision.

cards.title: Cross the Engineers' Line
cards.content: Life-safety work, strikebreaking, technical conscience, and command authority must be separated explicitly before Starfleet personnel enter a stopped facility.

cards.title: Who May Give the Order
cards.content: The player can recognize, reform, guarantee, temporarily administer, or decline to create a successor institution. The choice must specify authority and limits.

cards.title: Open Rhee's Quarters
cards.content: Mission relevance does not erase privacy. Pause on witnesses, scope, family process, emergency authority, and how the crew understands the succession.

cards.title: Accept or Contest Replacement
cards.content: If Starfleet offers a permanent replacement, present lawful options: accept, request delay, contest through review, define a transition, or demonstrate why continuity is operationally necessary.

cards.title: Distribute Command
cards.content: The finale requires the player to allocate ship systems, senior staff, shuttles, technical teams, and allies across simultaneous fronts. Delegation must use earned competence and authority.

cards.title: Nasrin Rhee - Public Role
cards.content: A respected, cautious captain who treats negotiated access and technical patience as command tools. She leads the opening shuttle evacuation and is killed after its passengers transport clear. Voice: Measured, economical, and attentive to who must live with a decision after the ship leaves. Rhee asks what can be defended publicly and what cannot yet be known.

cards.title: Nasrin Rhee - Director Notes
cards.content: Pressure hooks: The crew invokes her precedent; Her logs complicate disclosure; Her death makes ordinary captaincy rituals politically charged. Reveal gates: Her suspicion that the public lattice model omitted categories appears only through recovered logs, correspondence, or testimony.; Her agreement to delay a review in exchange for medical access to Nacre emerges after the player has independent evidence of the hidden burden.. Rhee dies in the opening after the shuttle passengers transport clear. Her death is not a hidden roll and must carry operational and personal weight. Her records provide evidence, not posthumous instructions.

cards.title: Asha Ren - Public Role
cards.content: Organized, direct, and politically perceptive. Ren understands how operational schedules become power and is the strongest default candidate to become Acting XO. Voice: Clear, compressed, and unsentimental about institutional language. Ren identifies who owns a process, who is excluded, and what decision is being disguised as administration.

cards.title: Asha Ren - Director Notes
cards.content: Pressure hooks: Must run the ship while the player governs the system; Challenges informal commitments that lack implementation; May decline or redefine the acting XO role. Reveal gates: Her worry that accepting the acting XO billet will be read as loyalty without independence surfaces after the first major disagreement with the player.. Ren supports the player's lawful succession but will not become a compliant echo. If another acting XO is chosen, she remains Operations Chief and a consequential alternative center of competence.

cards.title: Haro ch'Veth - Public Role
cards.content: Energetic, empirical, and impatient with ceremonial assurances. He can model the lattice at system scale but dislikes treating uncertainty as political permission to conceal data. Voice: Fast, precise, and openly skeptical. ch'Veth separates measurements from policy and states confidence levels even when the room wants certainty.

cards.title: Haro ch'Veth - Director Notes
cards.content: Pressure hooks: Advocates broad telemetry access; Disputes secrecy with Fenn and Osei; Can overvalue elegant models. Reveal gates: His tendency to underestimate the lethal transition between disclosure and implementation becomes visible after data publication changes behavior faster than infrastructure can adapt.. ch'Veth can prove disparities before he can prove the original fraud. Do not let him know the twelve-year rotation without evidence.

cards.title: Milo Fenn - Public Role
cards.content: An imaginative systems architect with a dry manner. Fenn sees the lattice as one machine distributed across five societies and is cautious about destabilizing it faster than alternatives can be built. Voice: Dry, concrete, and diagram-minded. Fenn turns policy into load paths, failure margins, fabrication hours, and the location where a promised compromise will physically break.

cards.title: Milo Fenn - Director Notes
cards.content: Pressure hooks: Needs scarce feedstock; Argues for staged change; Must decide whether to disclose his prior certification before others find it. Reveal gates: He helped certify a lattice modernization six years earlier and failed to identify the permanent cost shift.; His signature appears in records only after the relevant certification chain is recovered.. Fenn did not knowingly certify fraud. His review relied on curated categories. His accountability is real but distinct from deliberate concealment.

cards.title: Ila Tovan - Public Role
cards.content: Measured and attentive to population effects. Tovan treats environmental allocation as triage and resists technical language that hides who becomes sick, displaced, or infertile. Voice: Quiet, exact, and difficult to deflect. Tovan distinguishes reversible discomfort, chronic harm, acute mortality, and political embarrassment.

cards.title: Ila Tovan - Director Notes
cards.content: Pressure hooks: Nacre registries are incomplete; Medical supplies compete with processor parts; Can refuse to certify a lethal allocation as safe. Reveal gates: Her joined memory includes a prior public-health cover-up, but she does not invoke it as automatic moral authority.; She will disclose her concern only when the player asks her to endorse a mortality-obscuring allocation.. Tovan does not decide political legitimacy. She can make mortality and health consequences impossible to hide.

cards.title: Koris zh'Raal - Public Role
cards.content: Disciplined, restrained, and experienced in civil disorder. zh'Raal expects sabotage and coercion to increase but does not want the Eudora Vale used as an occupying police force. Voice: Controlled, specific, and procedural. zh'Raal distinguishes a dangerous person, a dangerous crowd, and an authority trying to call dissent a security problem.

cards.title: Koris zh'Raal - Director Notes
cards.content: Pressure hooks: Node seizures blur sabotage and labor action; Local security requests tactical backing; Evidence custody can conflict with rescue. Reveal gates: Her wartime experience with an allied security service that staged provocations surfaces after the first suspiciously convenient attack.. zh'Raal may recommend force but does not seek occupation. She should identify less coercive security options when they are operationally credible.

cards.title: Jaya Kel - Public Role
cards.content: Calm, practical, and highly familiar with orbital traffic. Kel planned the shuttle corridor used in the opening evacuation and responds to Rhee's death by trying to control every variable. Voice: Brief, spatial, and practical. Kel describes vectors, windows, traffic behavior, and the one assumption in a plan most likely to become false.

cards.title: Jaya Kel - Director Notes
cards.content: Pressure hooks: Conditional routes become political chokepoints; She may resist delegating shuttle work; Orbital demonstrations complicate safe transit. Reveal gates: Her private belief that a different approach vector might have saved Rhee surfaces only after another pilot or the player challenges her overcontrol.. The opening flight plan was reasonable under known data. Kel's guilt is emotional and professional, not proof of negligence.

cards.title: Venn Talar - Public Role
cards.content: Candid, low-key, and skilled at group process. Talar warns that system consensus often means the most powerful worlds agree with one another. Voice: Plain, patient, and lightly ironic. Talar names emotional facts without claiming telepathic certainty and asks who is absent from the room.

cards.title: Venn Talar - Director Notes
cards.content: Pressure hooks: Crew needs space for Rhee; Summits can become theater; Telepathic boundaries matter during high emotion. Reveal gates: His discomfort with institutional consensus comes from a failed mediation in which excluded workers later sabotaged a settlement, revealed only through earned rapport.. Talar can sense broad affect with consent and species limits. He cannot read campaign secrets or substitute empathy for evidence.

cards.title: U.S.S. Eudora Vale
cards.content: The Intrepid-class ship assigned to review the Ilyra Accord, coordinate emergency response, and provide independent scientific and diplomatic support. Everyday life: Crew move around a sealed captain's ready room, environmental specialists maintain rolling load plots in astrometrics, and the industrial replicator ledger is read at every senior briefing.

cards.title: U.S.S. Eudora Vale - Director Use
cards.content: The campaign's mobile command center. Its advanced sensors and laboratories can understand the lattice, but its damaged main deflector and limited fabrication stores prevent it from replacing regional infrastructure. Rhee's death turns every ready-room, bridge-watch, and personnel decision into part of the player's new captaincy.

cards.title: Crown Station
cards.content: The Accord's public control center and diplomatic hub, positioned between Aurelia and Pelagos. Everyday life: School groups watch weather maps in the public gallery, engineers pass through access gates beneath political banners, and every delegation maintains a separate room full of private calculations.

cards.title: Crown Station - Director Use
cards.content: Crown Station hosts traffic control, the Accord Secretariat, public telemetry, hearings, and visiting delegations. Its displays omit several categories of burden by design. It is both essential infrastructure and the stage on which continuity presents itself as transparency.

cards.title: Aurelia
cards.content: The Accord's temperate political and agricultural center, with the system's largest population and most visible public institutions. Everyday life: Weather bulletins are civic ritual, market prices respond to humidity forecasts, and children are taught that every loaf of bread is a gift from five worlds.

cards.title: Aurelia - Director Use
cards.content: Aurelia imports water vapor, exports waste heat, and benefits from the appearance that its temperate climate is the natural center of the system. Its population is not uniformly privileged: food workers, dry-belt settlements, and urban ration districts bear real risk when allocations change.

cards.title: Solis Terraces
cards.content: A continent-scale agricultural district on Aurelia whose climate towers feed much of the Ilyra System. Everyday life: Cooperative crews ride maintenance skiffs between climate towers, harvest forecasts are announced like election results, and emergency shelters double as seed banks.

cards.title: Solis Terraces - Director Use
cards.content: The opening surge exposes how tightly Aurelia's food security depends on imported Pelagic moisture and exported heat. Repairing the terraces without changing allocation perpetuates the old system; allowing losses creates immediate hunger and political backlash.

cards.title: Pelagos
cards.content: An ocean world of floating cities, desalination platforms, and deep-water ecosystems that supplies water vapor and biological catalysts to the lattice. Everyday life: Communities move with storm lanes, public assemblies meet on linked flotillas, and families track water-export quotas beside tide predictions.

cards.title: Pelagos - Director Use
cards.content: Pelagos considers water a sovereign contribution, not an infinite reservoir. Its official population model excludes seasonal and mobile communities, making export obligations larger than leaders admit. Reformers support Nacre while fearing Pelagos will become the next sacrifice world.

cards.title: Caldera Array
cards.content: A deep-water desalination and vapor-transfer complex that controls a large share of Pelagos' contribution to the lattice. Everyday life: Divers work in rotating pressure teams, shift changes are marked by sonar chimes, and civic observers can watch every export valve from a glass control gallery.

cards.title: Caldera Array - Director Use
cards.content: The Array can buffer exports, but doing so changes sea level and storm behavior. It stores old census assumptions in operational code. Pelagic technicians can help rewrite the model if protected from political blame.

cards.title: Viridia
cards.content: A forested world with exceptional biodiversity and a small population that supplies much of the lattice's biological processing capacity. Everyday life: Settlements are built around migration corridors, local weather reports include pollinator counts, and visitors are expected to justify every sealed road and cleared landing area.

cards.title: Viridia - Director Use
cards.content: Viridia acts as the system's living scrubber. Its conservation culture supports equity but rejects fixes that industrialize ecosystems or treat biodiversity as consumable infrastructure. Some conservators will sabotage projects they believe cross irreversible thresholds.

cards.title: Oros Valley Reserve
cards.content: A protected Viridian valley containing rare biomes used as a reference baseline for the entire planet. Everyday life: Field stations operate on minimal power, research crews travel on elevated paths, and conservation votes are held beside long-term species maps rather than in administrative halls.

cards.title: Oros Valley Reserve - Director Use
cards.content: Oros Valley can be quarantined from lattice processing, preserving ecological continuity while shifting chemical load elsewhere. It is the clearest place to demonstrate that equal tonnage is not equal harm.

cards.title: Ferrum
cards.content: A cold, metal-rich industrial world that receives heat and oxygen in exchange for components needed across the system. Everyday life: Shift assemblies are broadcast from factory floors, heat credits appear on household accounts, and every civic building contains an emergency oxygen room.

cards.title: Ferrum - Director Use
cards.content: Ferrum's worker councils know the lattice makes their lives possible and gives their fabrication labor political leverage. They distrust comfortable-world environmental rhetoric that treats Ferrum machinery as emergency inventory while excluding workers from decisions.

cards.title: Khepri City
cards.content: Ferrum's largest enclosed city and fabrication center, dependent on lattice heat and oxygen exchange. Everyday life: Residents carry compact respirators, children learn pressure-door drills, and union notices share walls with production targets and memorial plaques.

cards.title: Khepri City - Director Use
cards.content: Khepri can manufacture processor frames, shield components, and orbital supports if the Combine commits labor. School habitats and residential sectors share heat loops with industrial lines, making any shutdown morally and politically complex.

cards.title: Nacre
cards.content: A clouded world of sealed cities, corrosive rain, and expanding toxic seas whose maintenance culture keeps difficult environmental systems operating. Everyday life: Children identify rain chemistry by sound on shield roofs, public art is etched into replaceable corrosion panels, and maintenance shifts are treated as civic service.

cards.title: Nacre - Director Use
cards.content: Nacre has absorbed imported pollutants for generations. Its people possess the system's deepest practical maintenance knowledge and the least political power. The Nacre Assembly contains moderates, secessionists, and direct-action cells; no one group speaks for the whole world.

cards.title: Harmonic Spindle
cards.content: The primary energy and atmospheric-distribution node linking Viridia, Ferrum, and Nacre. Everyday life: Multiplanet crews work in mixed teams, every control surface displays three competing safety margins, and access corridors are lined with the names of technicians killed keeping the node online.

cards.title: Harmonic Spindle - Director Use
cards.content: The Spindle can reroute heat, gases, and atmospheric mass rapidly, but every emergency adjustment creates delayed consequences. Whoever controls its authorization keys can make temporary decisions with system-wide political force.

cards.title: Umbra Manifold
cards.content: A deep-orbit complex near Nacre that compresses toxic compounds and exports stabilized material. Everyday life: Crews sleep in rotating clean rooms, labels are engraved rather than printed, and maintenance apprentices memorize evacuation routes before they learn the control software.

cards.title: Umbra Manifold - Director Use
cards.content: Umbra contains obsolete processors, dangerous maintenance zones, original Accord records, and the best opportunity to build a genuine closed-cycle replacement. Some physical archives survived because Nacre technicians never trusted central databases.

cards.title: Ilyra Habitat Six
cards.content: A growing orbital settlement outside the five-world Accord that becomes an emergency transfer and fabrication point. Everyday life: Residents reuse construction modules as homes, communal kitchens face the system map, and every civic debate begins with whether temporary people are allowed permanent rights.

cards.title: Ilyra Habitat Six - Director Use
cards.content: Habitat Six began as a temporary construction camp and was excluded from the Accord's representation formulas. It can provide evacuation capacity and neutral meeting space, but recognition raises the question of whether the successor system has five members or six.

cards.title: Starfleet Ilyra Review Mission
cards.content: The Starfleet command responsible for the Eudora Vale's technical review, emergency response, and liaison with Federation authorities. Goals: Prevent mass casualties; Maintain freedom of navigation; Produce an independent technical record; Avoid Federation occupation.

cards.title: Starfleet Ilyra Review Mission - Director Use
cards.content: Rear Admiral Osei wants no mass-casualty event, no occupation, and a settlement that survives review. Starfleet is willing to grant discretion when the player documents risks, but worries that unilateral recognition or disclosure will create precedents elsewhere. Methods: Mission orders; Technical support; Command review; Emergency legal authority. Initial posture: supportive-but-watchful.

cards.title: Ilyra Accord Secretariat
cards.content: The interworld institution that administers the terraforming lattice and represents continuity of the five-world Accord. Goals: Keep the lattice operating; Preserve five-world unity; Prevent panic; Retain central authority.

cards.title: Ilyra Accord Secretariat - Director Use
cards.content: The Secretariat contains competent administrators, frightened continuity advocates, and officials who knowingly maintain curated telemetry. Vorr did not create the original fraud, but he chose to preserve concealment after learning enough to understand its political purpose. Methods: Allocation orders; Public telemetry; Emergency regulations; Diplomatic procedure. Initial posture: cooperative-and-controlling.

cards.title: Nacre Assembly
cards.content: Nacre's elected coalition seeking medical support, control over local nodes, public recognition of environmental harm, and a binding role in any successor system. Goals: End permanent burden; Gain node authority; Secure reparations; Protect Nacre lives.

cards.title: Nacre Assembly - Director Use
cards.content: The Assembly includes staged reformers, immediate secessionists, labor delegates, public-health advocates, and families who have stopped believing promises. It can cooperate if authority is real rather than consultative. Methods: Assembly resolutions; Labor action; Public testimony; Node control. Initial posture: exhausted-and-demanding.

cards.title: Lattice Engineers' Union
cards.content: The cross-world union whose technicians possess the practical knowledge required to operate, repair, or redesign the lattice. Goals: Protect workers; Secure technical honesty; Gain governance rights; Keep essential systems alive.

cards.title: Lattice Engineers' Union - Director Use
cards.content: The Union is the system's most credible cross-world institution but has been used as a politically neutral instrument. Its strike threat is both labor action and a claim that no allocation is legitimate without the people who execute it. Methods: Work rules; Coordinated maintenance; Certification refusal; Strike action. Initial posture: cautious-and-essential.

cards.title: Pelagic Council
cards.content: Pelagos' elected interflotilla council, focused on water sovereignty and protection against becoming the next environmental sink. Goals: Protect water reserves; Correct population records; Limit open-ended export duties; Preserve Pelagic autonomy.

cards.title: Pelagic Council - Director Use
cards.content: The Council supports Nacre's claim in principle while using procedure to extract enforceable caps. It is internally divided between mobile communities, array operators, and city blocs. Methods: Quota negotiation; Export throttling; Census challenges; Coalition politics. Initial posture: wary-and-procedural.

cards.title: Ferrum Combine
cards.content: A federation of worker councils and industrial cooperatives capable of supplying much of the hardware needed for lattice reconstruction. Goals: Keep Ferrum habitable; Protect labor rights; Secure allocation guarantees; Control industrial priorities.

cards.title: Ferrum Combine - Director Use
cards.content: The Combine can mobilize fabrication or a system-wide stoppage. It will cooperate when treated as a governing partner and resist when labor is requisitioned as if it were Starfleet inventory. Methods: Fabrication schedules; Worker councils; Collective bargaining; Production stoppage. Initial posture: pragmatic-and-suspicious.

cards.title: Viridian Conservancy
cards.content: The scientific and civic coalition responsible for protecting Viridia's biodiversity and biological processing reserve. Goals: Preserve biodiversity; Limit chemical pulses; Require ecological accounting; Prevent industrial capture.

cards.title: Viridian Conservancy - Director Use
cards.content: The Conservancy supports equity but rejects fixes that make Viridia a less visible sacrifice. A radical minority will damage industrial projects it believes threaten irreversible ecological loss. Methods: Scientific review; Protected zones; Public injunctions; Direct action by a minority. Initial posture: supportive-but-defensive.

cards.title: Accord Security Service
cards.content: The interworld security organization responsible for node protection, traffic control, and investigation of sabotage. Goals: Prevent node seizure; Preserve public order; Maintain access control; Protect the Secretariat.

cards.title: Accord Security Service - Director Use
cards.content: Most officers are legitimate public-safety personnel. Director Orin and a small continuity cell stage one false-flag incident to justify emergency control. The Service can fracture, reform, or become an enforcement arm depending on how evidence is handled. Methods: Security cordons; Investigations; Traffic restrictions; Emergency warrants. Initial posture: formal-and-escalating.

cards.title: Helioform Consortium
cards.content: A private climate-engineering consortium offering rapid orbital processors, financing, and maintenance contracts. Goals: Win the processor concession; Control proprietary telemetry; Secure long-term maintenance rights; Avoid liability.

cards.title: Helioform Consortium - Director Use
cards.content: Helioform can deliver real short-term capacity. Its price is long-term control of processing infrastructure, proprietary telemetry, and priority repayment clauses that favor politically stable worlds. Methods: Technical demonstrations; Credit guarantees; Lobbying; Exclusive contracts. Initial posture: helpful-and-opportunistic.

cards.title: Unburdened Network
cards.content: A loose Nacre direct-action network demanding immediate transfer of node control and an end to unconsented load imports. Goals: End imported burden immediately; Force recognition; Protect Nacre technicians; Break Secretariat control.

cards.title: Unburdened Network - Director Use
cards.content: Some cells sabotage relays; others protect protesters, leak records, or maintain essential systems during seizures. They do not share one leader or complete system knowledge. Treating every cell as terrorist organization strengthens the most reckless wing. Methods: Leaks; Occupations; Relay sabotage; Mutual aid. Initial posture: fragmented-and-escalating.

cards.title: Rear Admiral Celeste Osei
cards.content: The admiral overseeing the Ilyra review. She supports documented extraordinary action but has little tolerance for unilateral occupation or avoidable public chaos. Director context: Osei knows the review was politically sensitive and that Rhee had requested broader medical access. She does not know the original rotation or false flag at campaign start. She may offer a replacement captain if scrutiny, ship damage, or political fracture becomes severe. Knowledge boundary: Knows Starfleet orders and Rhee's formal reports; Knows the lattice is under strain; Does not know the concealed rotation or full Nacre load.

cards.title: First Minister Elian Vorr
cards.content: Aurelia's senior statesman and public face of the Accord, intelligent and convinced that continuity must survive long enough for reform to work. Director context: Vorr discovered four years ago that public categories hid imported burden and chose not to expose it without a transition plan. He knows concealment is morally compromised and believes sudden truth could kill millions. Knowledge boundary: Knows the dashboards omit categories; Knows Nacre is carrying disproportionate load; Does not possess every original engineering record or understand every shutdown consequence.

cards.title: Speaker Mara Senn
cards.content: A former maintenance engineer whose patience with promises is exhausted. She can support staged reform only if Nacre receives real authority and enforceable limits. Director context: Senn knows local telemetry, health effects, and the history of failed petitions. She does not know the complete system model and cannot guarantee control of every direct-action cell. Knowledge boundary: Knows Nacre's maintenance data and political history; Knows some Unburdened organizers; Does not know the full immediate shutdown consequences.

cards.title: Convenor Kesh Var
cards.content: A veteran systems engineer who insists technicians are political actors whenever they are ordered to decide who receives air, water, or heat. Director context: Var knows certifications were altered and that many engineers signed under pressure. He can coordinate a system-wide repair effort or strike, but will not expose members without protection and a credible accountability process. Knowledge boundary: Knows practical load paths and altered certifications; Knows the Union's internal dissent; Does not know all Secretariat political decisions.

cards.title: Councillor Rian Oso
cards.content: A patient negotiator who supports Nacre's case but refuses open-ended water obligations or formulas based on populations Pelagos knows are undercounted. Director context: Oso uses delay as leverage because Pelagos has repeatedly been promised later safeguards. He will move quickly when offered enforceable caps, corrected census data, and appeal rights. Knowledge boundary: Knows Pelagic census and water-export discrepancies; Knows Council factions; Does not know the original rotation documents.

cards.title: Foreperson Dela Marr
cards.content: A worker-council leader who can mobilize fabrication capacity or a system-wide stoppage and judges outsiders by whether they treat labor as partners. Director context: Marr knows Ferrum leaders threatened withdrawal during the first attempted rotation and carries inherited shame about the decision. She will support truth if Ferrum is not simply assigned a new unbounded sacrifice. Knowledge boundary: Knows Ferrum's dependency and production capacity; Knows labor history around the delayed rotation; Does not know the full dashboard redesign.

cards.title: Ecologist Sorel Thann
cards.content: A systems ecologist who argues that a mathematically equal allocation can still be ecologically suicidal. Director context: Thann knows the planet is near a non-linear threshold and suspects a radical cell has damaged an industrial test site. He will not conceal ecological danger to preserve an appearance of unity. Knowledge boundary: Knows Viridian threshold data; Knows Conservancy factions; Does not know the complete Nacre burden history.

cards.title: Director Cael Orin
cards.content: The professional head of Accord Security, requesting Starfleet support against sabotage while insisting local civil authority remain intact. Director context: Orin authorizes one false-flag relay attack through a small continuity cell to justify node-control powers. He believes temporary coercion will prevent a real shutdown. Most of the Service does not know. Knowledge boundary: Knows security investigations and the false-flag cell; Knows some Secretariat omissions; Does not know the exact original rotation or all Unburdened cells.

cards.title: Doctor Lyra Veen
cards.content: A public-health physician maintaining incomplete but devastating records of chronic illness across Nacre's sealed cities. Director context: Veen can establish long-term harm and mortality but has protected some patient data from both the Secretariat and militants. She will share under consent and custody safeguards. Knowledge boundary: Knows patient patterns and local exposure; Knows registry gaps; Does not know every source allocation or political decision.

cards.title: Avar Jess
cards.content: A Nacre nodekeeper associated with direct-action organizers who argue that continued unconsented operation is itself violence. Director context: Jess helps seize a node but continues essential services. Another cell, not Jess, destroys a relay. Jess has an incomplete system model and can be persuaded by credible shared authority. Knowledge boundary: Knows Nacre node procedures and some direct-action plans; Does not control every cell; Does not know full shutdown consequences.

cards.title: Alix Meral
cards.content: A persuasive climate engineer offering rapid orbital processors, financing, and a complete maintenance package. Director context: Meral's technology works, though not at the scale claimed without local components. The contract would create long-term control and data asymmetry. She is not sabotaging public alternatives; she benefits from their delay. Knowledge boundary: Knows consortium performance limits and contract terms; Knows public telemetry; Does not know hidden archives or false flag.

cards.title: Coordinator Nia Tess
cards.content: The elected coordinator of an orbital settlement excluded from the Accord despite becoming essential to construction and evacuation. Director context: Tess wants representation, not charity. Habitat Six can serve as neutral ground and evacuation capacity if recognized. It also contains undocumented workers who fear census exposure. Knowledge boundary: Knows habitat capacity and labor networks; Knows who the Accord excludes; Does not know the concealed rotation.

cards.title: Ines Quill
cards.content: A retired auditor who remained near Umbra Manifold after leaving the Secretariat under disputed circumstances. Director context: Quill preserved physical copies of the original rotation clause and knows how public categories were redesigned. She participated in the redesign under threat of system withdrawal and has waited for an authority she believes can protect the record. Knowledge boundary: Knows original rotation and dashboard redesign; Knows some names in the certification chain; Does not know current sabotage plans.

cards.title: Minister Talia Iven
cards.content: The official responsible for food security on Aurelia, under intense pressure to prevent crop loss and public panic. Director context: Iven knows Aurelia receives more moisture than public civic education implies. She does not know the full Nacre burden and will support transparent rationing if presented with credible system-wide support. Knowledge boundary: Knows Aurelia allocation and crop windows; Knows public sentiment; Does not know original rotation or security false flag.

cards.title: Lattice Cascade
cards.content: Accumulated damage, delayed loads, high-energy synchronization, and incompatible local interventions increase the chance of linked node failure.

cards.title: Lattice Cascade - Progression
cards.content: Goals: Shorten safe allocation windows; Create simultaneous crises; Force a final configuration. Stage cues: The lattice is strained but normal emergency procedures still work. | Allocation delays create visible weather and supply anomalies. | Two worlds experience linked emergencies and node margins narrow. | Conditional routes and processing nodes require active protection. | Several governments impose unilateral controls and the Spindle becomes contested. | A system-wide cascade is imminent; only distributed preparation can prevent catastrophic loss. | A Common Climate is underway across all five worlds.

cards.title: Nacre Secession
cards.content: Delay, coercion, untreated illness, and concealed data push Nacre from reform demands toward unilateral node control and withdrawal.

cards.title: Nacre Secession - Progression
cards.content: Goals: End unconsented load; Seize local nodes; Build independent authority. Stage cues: The Assembly demands a timetable and independent data access. | Public demonstrations and work-to-rule actions spread. | Direct-action cells interfere with relays and access controls. | Nodekeepers seize local operations while maintaining essential services. | The Assembly prepares a secession declaration and rejects Secretariat warrants. | Nacre begins unilateral shutdown or independent allocation. | Accord authority no longer operates on Nacre.

cards.title: Public Legitimacy Crisis
cards.content: Conflicting telemetry, curated statements, visible sacrifice, and Starfleet intervention determine whether populations see decisions as lawful and intelligible.

cards.title: Public Legitimacy Crisis - Progression
cards.content: Goals: Expose contradictions; Create parallel narratives; Force public accountability. Stage cues: Most residents still trust the Accord while asking difficult questions. | Independent data challenges official briefings. | Public hearings become confrontational and local governments issue separate forecasts. | One or more worlds reject Crown Station telemetry. | Emergency orders lack broad compliance and protests disrupt traffic. | Parallel authorities claim lawful control. | No shared public account of the crisis remains.

cards.title: Engineers' Mobilization
cards.content: The Lattice Engineers' Union moves from protected technical dissent toward coordinated certification refusal and strike action.

cards.title: Engineers' Mobilization - Progression
cards.content: Goals: Protect technicians; Gain governance rights; Stop unsafe or deceptive orders. Stage cues: Union delegates demand protected review and honest telemetry. | Work rules slow nonessential maintenance. | Certification refusals delay new allocations. | Minimum-safety staffing replaces normal operations. | A system-wide strike begins outside immediate life support. | Local governments attempt requisition or replacement labor. | The lattice loses cross-world technical coordination.

cards.title: Resource Scarcity
cards.content: Fabrication feedstock, water buffers, medical supplies, emergency power, and shuttle capacity are consumed faster than ordinary replenishment.

cards.title: Resource Scarcity - Progression
cards.content: Goals: Force prioritization; Create dependency on local partners; Make relief decisions persistent. Stage cues: The Eudora Vale and local governments retain workable reserves. | Several planned repairs compete for the same material. | Worlds begin withholding reserves for domestic use. | Medical, habitat, and processor needs cannot all be met. | Emergency rationing and contract offers reshape alliances. | At least one world loses a critical buffer. | The finale begins with severe material shortages.

cards.title: Security Escalation
cards.content: Sabotage, false attribution, node occupations, and traffic restrictions normalize coercive control around the lattice.

cards.title: Security Escalation - Progression
cards.content: Goals: Expand emergency powers; Militarize node control; Turn political conflict into security categories. Stage cues: Security incidents remain isolated and locally managed. | Access checks and patrols increase around major nodes. | Sabotage claims outpace verified evidence. | Traffic cordons and mass detentions become politically plausible. | Armed forces contest node access and protect rival authorities. | The Harmonic Spindle is treated as strategic territory. | Violence determines allocation unless interrupted.

cards.title: Ecological Overshoot
cards.content: Chemical pulses, simplified bioprocessing, storm shifts, and concentrated load push living systems toward irreversible thresholds.

cards.title: Ecological Overshoot - Progression
cards.content: Goals: Make delayed ecological costs visible; Constrain purely technical optimization; Create nonhuman stakes. Stage cues: Ecological systems remain strained but recoverable. | Localized die-offs and chemical blooms appear. | Viridia and Pelagos lose important biological buffers. | Emergency interventions simplify ecosystems faster than they recover. | At least one world approaches an irreversible threshold. | The final stabilization will permanently damage a major ecosystem unless alternatives exist. | Ecological collapse becomes part of the post-campaign reality.

cards.title: Captaincy Review
cards.content: Ship condition, candor, legal judgment, crew confidence, and political controversy determine whether Starfleet confirms the player's first command.

cards.title: Captaincy Review - Progression
cards.content: Goals: Assess command continuity; Trigger outside intervention if needed; Shape the epilogue. Stage cues: The player holds ordinary acting authority after lawful succession. | Starfleet requests more frequent command reports. | A formal field-confirmation review begins. | A replacement captain is made available or inquiry conditions are imposed. | The player's authority is contested before the finale. | Final command disposition awaits the campaign outcome.

cards.title: The Captain's Chair
cards.content: The prelude establishes ordinary XO competence, Rhee's death, lawful succession, damaged deflector capacity, the first persistent allocation consequence, and the campaign rule that every rescue shifts pressure somewhere else. The cause is genuinely uncertain at first; do not force sabotage as the opening answer. Dramatic question: What kind of captain does the player become when their first command decision must make one population carry a cost? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: The opening succession and first system-wide allocation cannot be delegated.

cards.title: Bread and Weather
cards.content: This quest makes Aurelia sympathetic and privileged at the same time. It should reveal real food risk, the world's imported moisture dependence, and the political habit of treating Aurelian stability as system stability. The player can save crops without preserving every existing allocation. Dramatic question: How much immediate food security may one world demand from a system whose old guarantees concealed their cost? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: The first Aurelian intervention sets a major allocation precedent and requires captain-level authority.

cards.title: The Weight of Water
cards.content: This quest establishes Pelagos as both donor and vulnerable society. The census discrepancy is not a clerical twist; it reflects mobile communities repeatedly excluded from fixed-world representation. Water sovereignty can become an asset or a wedge. Dramatic question: Who counts when a system calculates how much a world can safely give away? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: The export precedent and population recognition require direct command responsibility.

cards.title: Borrowed Breath
cards.content: Ferrum should feel neither selfish nor reducible to a factory. The quest reveals its existential dependence, industrial leverage, and labor governance. The player can earn fabrication support only by treating workers as political partners. Dramatic question: When survival and production share the same heat loop, who has authority to decide which one counts as essential? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: The allocation and labor-authority precedent require the captain.

cards.title: The Green Lung
cards.content: This quest establishes ecology as a full ending axis. It should make clear that equal mass does not produce equal harm, and that Viridian resistance can be principled without being consequence-free. Dramatic question: Can a living world be treated as infrastructure without reducing it to a consumable machine? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: The ecological allocation decision cannot be reduced to a delegated technical task.

cards.title: The Public Model
cards.content: Unlock after any three of the four world quests or equivalent evidence. The scene is not one terminal-room reveal; it is a conflict over access, verification, custody, and who may certify the system's truth. Critical evidence has routes through world data, Union records, Rhee, or Umbra. Dramatic question: Who gets to verify a system whose official transparency has become part of the deception? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: The audit can use delegated technical teams, but evidence custody and disclosure authority require the captain.

cards.title: Twelve Years
cards.content: This quest reveals the original mandatory twelve-year rotation, the first deferral, and the redesign of public categories. It must not be the only evidence route: the Cold Library, Rhee's records, Fenn's certification, and Union testimony can establish portions if Umbra is lost. Dramatic question: What does accountability require when the system's founding promise was not merely broken but edited out of public memory? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: Custody and disclosure of foundational evidence require direct command judgment.

cards.title: The Daylight Protocol
cards.content: This is a command and public-legitimacy quest, not a binary publish/suppress choice. The player can sequence disclosure, share custody, set a timetable, protect vulnerable infrastructure, or let local institutions lead. Every route should clearly state foreseeable risks. Dramatic question: Can truth be staged without becoming another form of control? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: Disclosure timing and Starfleet accountability are captain-level decisions.

cards.title: Rain Country
cards.content: This quest turns Nacre into a full recurring hub and grounds the hidden burden in ordinary life. It should offer rescue, medicine, engineering, diplomacy, and crew interaction without making the world only a site of suffering. Dramatic question: What does recognition look like before a treaty exists, when local competence is already keeping people alive? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: The first formal relationship with Nacre and emergency resource tradeoff require the captain.

cards.title: The Fifth Share
cards.content: The seized node is not a hostage device. The occupiers are competent and preserve life support. The conflict is about lawful control, practical legitimacy, and whether Starfleet treats Nacre as a coequal government before a final charter exists. Dramatic question: When existing law rests on concealed sacrifice, what makes emergency authority legitimate? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: Political recognition, rules of engagement, and use of Starfleet force require the captain.

cards.title: Many Hands
cards.content: This quest prevents the campaign from collapsing into a mastermind plot. At least four causal chains exist: a direct-action relay attack, a Security false flag, Viridian sabotage of an industrial proposal, and genuine material failure. The player may prove some but not all. Dramatic question: Can justice remain specific when every institution benefits from calling all damage the work of one enemy? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: The investigation may be distributed, but the public accountability policy and security boundaries require direct command.

cards.title: Terms of Survival
cards.content: This is a portfolio-design quest rather than a single summit. The player should visit or communicate with multiple worlds, combine assets, and state a clear decision rule for future emergencies. A technically good plan without consent remains fragile; a fair plan without capacity may fail. Dramatic question: What temporary inequality can be justified when the alternative is immediate collapse, and who gets to define temporary? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: The interim decision rule defines captain-level authority and cannot be delegated as a technical recommendation.

cards.title: Closed Cycle
cards.content: This quest reads campaign assets rather than assuming them. Missing assets do not block play; they change scale, risk, cost, and who controls the resulting infrastructure. The player can build a public network, lease a private one, construct partial redundancy, or abandon the attempt. Dramatic question: Can infrastructure become common property before the crisis forces someone else to own it? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: Subtasks can be delegated, but architecture, ownership, and ship-risk decisions require the captain.

cards.title: The Engineers' Line
cards.content: The strike is not a sabotage plot. Minimum life-support work can continue, but coordination and new allocations slow sharply. The player can negotiate governance, requisition labor, support a protected strike, or build an alternate emergency workforce at real cost. Dramatic question: Can essential workers exercise collective power without being told that survival cancels their rights? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: The labor-authority and emergency-law decision require the captain.

cards.title: Broken Accord
cards.content: This is the political convergence, assembled from Nacre standing, disclosure, Union status, world relationships, security posture, and side-quest assets. A summit is one approach, not the required scene. The player may recognize a provisional council, reform the Secretariat, establish temporary Starfleet trusteeship, or manage separation. Dramatic question: What lawful authority can replace an agreement whose legitimacy depended on hiding its cost? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: The authority and Starfleet-role decision cannot be delegated.

cards.title: A Common Climate
cards.content: The finale is assembled from actual state. Select pressures from Nacre toxic rain, Aurelia crop failure, Ferrum freeze, Pelagic storm surge, Viridian die-off, Spindle seizure, orbital collision, ship damage, Union status, and political authority. Do not present every possible crisis if prior play prevented it. Dramatic question: Can the system survive without making one world invisible again? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: The finale is distributed through delegation but remains the player captain's command.

cards.title: Weather We Share
cards.content: The epilogue reads the ending family and campaign axes. It should name surviving institutions, ongoing costs, damaged relationships, and commitments rather than declaring abstract victory. Command confirmation follows judgment, candor, crew confidence, ship condition, and lawful process. Dramatic question: What does accountability require after the emergency has passed and the consequences remain? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: The command review is the player's epilogue decision.

cards.title: Rain at Noon
cards.content: The assignment is a focused municipal emergency. City crews know the shield network but lack replacement emitters. The Eudora Vale can divert industrial feedstock, borrow from Umbra, accept narrower protection, or evacuate exposed districts. The decision must persist as a resource and relationship consequence rather than becoming a disposable rescue vignette. Dramatic question: When every spare part already belongs to another promised future, whose immediate danger receives it? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: A qualified engineering-medical team can lead the repair, but consuming promised parts requires explicit command authorization.

cards.title: Salt Debt
cards.content: The export formula counts some abandoned districts and omits newer floating cities, producing obligations that are numerically precise and politically obsolete. The player can conduct a census, establish provisional quotas, recognize city-level bargaining, or enforce the old ledger during emergency. This side quest should inform later water assets and legitimacy without secretly becoming the whole rotation plot. Dramatic question: When a shared obligation is measured by an obsolete record, is compliance justice or merely precision? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: A delegated audit can succeed, but force, quota, and representation choices require command guidance.

cards.title: The Children of Ferrum
cards.content: The school is physically connected to an industrial district whose workers have stopped nonessential operations. Security and management claim the strike caused the loss; union logs show a deferred maintenance failure. The player can provide direct aid, ask union crews to treat the school as life-safety work, compel access, or relocate children. The assignment should remain about labor, safety, and care rather than revealing a hidden villain. Dramatic question: Can command save people without turning humanitarian aid into a weapon against lawful collective action? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: Delegation is appropriate if the rescue team has explicit instructions about strike neutrality and access.

cards.title: A Forest Under Glass
cards.content: The valley is not secretly a strategic site. It is a calm scientific and ethical assignment with real regional consequences. Quarantine requires a substitute processing path, reduced system demand, or acceptance of short-term load elsewhere. The player may authorize a limited preserve, collect a seed and microbiome archive, build a transparent enclosure, or refuse. Direct action by extremists should not intrude unless current campaign state causally brings it here. Dramatic question: What does preservation mean when the protected place still depends on the wider system that is harming it? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: Delegation is safe if substitute load and local consent are already defined. The assignment should remain calm unless current state requires otherwise.

cards.title: The Engineer's Oath
cards.content: The records show a spectrum: some engineers knowingly falsified, some accepted narrow scopes, some trusted curated data, and some objected privately. Fenn missed the permanent cost shift in a Federation-assisted review. This is a crew and institutional accountability quest, not a courtroom with one correct verdict. The player controls duty assignments, evidence handling, immediate suspensions, and support for disclosure. Dramatic question: How should command use expertise that is necessary, compromised, and capable of telling the truth now? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: Evidence review can be delegated; Fenn's duty status and the accountability framework are direct command responsibilities.

cards.title: Weather for Sale
cards.content: The technology is functional and can materially help. The danger is not sabotage but lock-in: proprietary maintenance, climate telemetry ownership, debt service, and the ability to prioritize paying jurisdictions. The player can accept, reject, renegotiate, license components, or use Helioform only as a temporary contractor. Local actors disagree based on urgent need and political sovereignty. Dramatic question: When a private rescue works, what makes accepting it different from selling future control of survival? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: Technical and legal review can be delegated, but operating control and debt commitments require explicit command and local authority.

cards.title: The Cold Library
cards.content: The archive offers a resilient evidence route but should not become the sole critical clue. Retrieval requires hazardous entry, local guides, containment, and choices between records, living evacuees, and structural stability. Some records are mundane civic history; preserving them matters beyond the rotation investigation. The archive can reveal the signed rotation schedule, early health data, and local engineering practice. Dramatic question: What deserves rescue from a place the system treated as expendable: proof, memory, or the people still maintaining both? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: A specialized team can lead recovery, but evidence-versus-life priorities and custody require command guidance.

cards.title: Rhee's Quarters
cards.content: This is a quiet shipboard command and mourning assignment. Rhee separated personal logs, command records, and privileged correspondence. The player can follow standard inventory, appoint witnesses, defer private material, seek family consent, or open mission-relevant files under emergency authority. Her records reveal suspicion and a bargain for Nacre medical access, but only when access and independent context justify it. Do not turn the scene into Rhee speaking posthumously with perfect answers. Dramatic question: What does command inherit from a dead captain, and what remains hers? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: Inventory work can be delegated, but command privacy, succession meaning, and emergency access must be decided by the player.

cards.title: The Sixth Habitat
cards.content: The habitat is neither a planet nor a temporary work camp. It has permanent residents, schools, clinics, maintenance capacity, and no vote in the Accord. During the crisis it absorbs evacuees and industrial traffic, increasing its importance and risk. The player can recognize a delegate, negotiate service obligations, expand life support, or treat it as a logistical facility. Recognition affects the successor charter and finale capacity. Dramatic question: When does a place become a political community rather than infrastructure serving someone else? Valid approaches are not limited to the authored examples. Delegation: Engineering support can be delegated, but representation and charter standing require explicit command and political engagement.

cards.title: A Common Climate
cards.content: All five populations retain viable futures, Nacre's permanent burden ends, shared authority is lawful, and redundant infrastructure exists. This is difficult, not pristine; name reparations, maintenance, and continuing ecological limits.

cards.title: Reformed Accord
cards.content: The system survives under a legitimate but unequal transition with hard caps, independent telemetry, rotation or review rules, and enforceable exit mechanisms.

cards.title: Five Separate Skies
cards.content: Shared political authority dissolves while warning, navigation, emergency aid, and controlled decommissioning remain coordinated. Sovereignty is gained at the cost of shortages, migration, and lower collective capacity.

cards.title: Peace by Allocation
cards.content: A Starfleet or Secretariat regime limits casualties through technical control without durable consent. Operational success and political failure must both remain visible.

cards.title: The Fifth World Falls
cards.content: Other worlds survive while Nacre suffers mass evacuation or ecological collapse. Do not describe this as victory; record survival, culpability, displaced communities, and the institutional narrative used afterward.

cards.title: Broken Worlds
cards.content: Multiple nodes collapse and at least two populations face catastrophic loss. The campaign concludes through evacuation command, triage, inquiry, and long-term obligations rather than a game-over abstraction.

cards.title: Command Outcome
cards.content: Captaincy disposition is separate from the regional ending. Confirmation, acting status, censure, relief, or voluntary transition follows crew confidence, candor, lawful process, ship risk, and Starfleet scrutiny.

canonEra.dominionWarStatus: The Dominion War has ended. Starfleet is rebuilding ships, institutions, and confidence while regional governments reassess wartime emergency arrangements.
canonEra.voyagerStatus: Voyager remains in the Delta Quadrant in 2378 until its canonical return later that year. It does not intersect the Eudora Vale campaign.
canonEra.canonBoundary: Canon factions, technology, and era conditions may provide background. The Eudora Vale, crew, Ilyra System, local governments, Accord history, and campaign outcomes are original and canon-adjacent.

missionDirectionRules: Situation on rails, solution off rails.
The opening succession, linked lattice, original rotation, curated dashboard, Nacre burden, and lethality of abrupt shutdown are fixed truths; methods and outcomes remain open.
Every crisis requires a causal parent in load transfer, material condition, elapsed time, actor choice, institutional order, sabotage, or prior committed consequence.
The Ilyra System is persistent world state; factions, worlds, engineers, weather, and shortages continue outside the foreground quest.
Some calm side assignments remain calm and unrelated to sabotage.
Do not collapse rescue authority, scientific custody, medical privacy, technical command, civil order, treaty standing, and constitutional legitimacy into one authority by default.
Crew disagreement should be focused and professional rather than a full-crew debate every scene.
No character begins with the complete campaign truth.
If the player bypasses an expected scene, move information only through evidence, testimony, telemetry, records, or actors with access.
Escalation follows established physical and political processes rather than arbitrary countdowns or mandatory combat.
Factions act between scenes according to goals, knowledge, capabilities, access, and prior treatment.
Delegated work uses earned assets, authority, expertise, and route access; it is never automatic success or failure.
Senior-staff briefings distinguish facts, inference, unknowns, risk, capacity, recommendations, and the command decision required.
Technical equality is not presumed ecological or political equality.
The player character alone determines their dialogue, intent, feelings, judgments, and decisions.
hiddenInformation: Raw campaign track values and exact front stages
The original twelve-year rotation before discovery
The redesign that hid imported burden in public categories
The exact distribution of responsibility for each sabotage incident
The Accord Security false flag before causal evidence
Rhee's private suspicion and medical-access compromise before lawful access
Fenn's prior certification record before recovery
Exact future weather and cascade queue
Predetermined valid solutions or Command Decisions
NPC loyalty, relationship, or hidden approval numbers
failurePolicy: No single failed check ends a mission.
Campaign-critical truths have multiple routes through telemetry, archives, public-health data, engineering records, witness testimony, Rhee's notes, and physical system behavior.
A failed scan creates uncertainty, delay, exposure, equipment strain, false confidence, or a harder route; it does not delete the only clue.
Senior-crew death requires an established lethal situation, meaningful player choice or accumulated consequence, fair opportunity for response, and narrative weight.
Refusing, postponing, delegating, withdrawing from, or abandoning an assignment is a consequential command act.
Delay advances fronts and clocks only when actors or physical processes have reason to continue.
The Eudora Vale may be heavily damaged, lose political standing, accept replacement command, or withdraw from a location without invalidating the campaign.
Severe endings continue through evacuation, inquiry, reconstruction, reparations, and surviving obligations rather than reset or game-over abstraction.
playerFacingInfo: Known route status, travel time, and environmental restrictions
Public deadlines and visible front consequences
Major ship conditions, shuttle availability, deflector restrictions, medical capacity, and fabrication limits
Current crew assignments and acting command structure
Named agreements, telemetry packages, local partners, processors, evacuation assets, and other earned campaign assets
